A USB flash drive (USB stands for "Universal Serial Bus") is a popular way to store digital information. Flash drives are an easy way to share data (information). A USB flash drive can be attached to a USB port, and provides a certain amount of storage space, which can be used to store data. USB flash drives are used with devices found in homes, workplaces and schools. Below are some examples of these devices: Title: How to Change the GMail Layout on Android Introduction: Gmail is one of the most popular email services used by millions of people around the world. Google frequently updates its design and layout to improve user experience, but sometimes these changes may not align with your preferences. Fortunately, you can customize the GMail layout on Android devices to suit your needs. This tutorial will guide you through the process step-by-step, providing clear explanations and helpful tips along the way. Step 1: Open the GMail App Start by unlocking your Android device and locating the GMail app icon. Tap on the icon to open the application. If you are not already logged into your account, enter your login credentials to proceed. Step 2: Access Settings Once you have successfully opened the GMail app, locate and tap on the three horizontal lines (hamburger menu) at the top left corner of the screen. A side panel will appear displaying various options such as All Inboxes, Primary, Social, Promotions, etc. Scroll down until you find "Settings" and tap on it. Key Tip: The hamburger menu provides quick access to all sections within the GMail app. Familiarizing yourself with this feature will help you navigate more efficiently. Step 3: Select Your Account After tapping on settings, a new page will load showing any accounts linked to your device that support GMail integration. Choose the account whose layout you wish to modify by simply tapping on it. Guideline: Make sure you select the correct account if multiple accounts are listed here. Changing the layout affects only the selected account. Step 4: Customize General Settings Upon selecting an account, another page will appear where you'll see several categories like 'General settings', 'Data usage,' 'Notifications,' etc. For changing the overall look of your mailbox, focus on the 'General settings.' Step 5: Adjust Density Underneath the general setting category, there should be an option labeled 'Density.' Tap on it, then choose between 'Comfortable,' 'Default,' or 'Compact' according to your preference. Compact mode displays more emails per scroll while comfortable mode increases readability. Step 6: Configure Swipe Actions Still under 'General settings,' find the subheading named 'Swipe actions.' Here, you can assign specific functions when swiping right or left over emails. Options include Archive, Delete, Mark as read/unread, Move to, Snooze, or None. Step 7: Enable Dark Theme (Optional) If your phone supports dark themes, you might want to enable them in GMail too. Look for 'Theme' under 'General settings' and select either 'Light,' 'Dark,' or 'System default.' Choosing 'Dark' gives your eyes rest during low light conditions. Step 8: Save Changes Remember to save any modifications made by scrolling up and hitting the 'Save changes' button located near the top of the screen. Conclusion: Changing the GMail layout on Android allows users to tailor their email experience according to personal preference. By following our comprehensive guide above, you now know how to adjust density, configure swipe actions, and even switch to a dark theme. Enjoy optimized productivity and enhanced usability with your newly customized GMail interface! Micky was a funny little mouse. He liked to do things differently. To learn to read and spell words, children need to know that letters stand for phonemes and spellings map out the phonemes in spoken words. Children must learn to recognize phonemes before they can match them to letters. This lesson will help children identify /i/ (short i). They will learn to recognize /i/ in spoken words as well as the letter representing it. Primary paper and pencil; chart with Sid, the Indian, is in his igloo ; drawing paper and crayons; picture page with bug, pin, kitten, tint, lips, dog, and chin. 1. Introduce the lesson by explaining that writing is a secret code. Today we re going to work on spotting the mouth move /i/. As you get to know /i/ it will be easy to spot in 2. Ask students: Have you ever put your hands in something gross and said this is icky . The beginning of icky is the mouth move that we ll be looking for today. Everybody say kitty and stretch it out. See if you hear the icky sound. Yes, right in the middle. 3. Let s try a tongue twister. (on chart) Sid the Indian is in his igloo. Everybody say it together. Say it again but this time stretch the /i/ at the beginning of the words. Sid the iiindian iiis iiin his iiigloo. Try it again, and this time break it off the word: Sid the /i/ ndian /i/ s /i/ n his /i/ gloo. Good work 4. We can use the letter i to spell /i/. Let s write it. Start at the fence line. Draw a line straight down to the sidewalk. Then right above the fence line, make a dot. After I check your letter i, I want you to make a whole row just like that one. 5. I m going to ask you some questions and raise your hand to answer. Do you hear /i/ in pin or bat? Sit or stand? Kitty or dog? Bit or eat? I am going to give you a card with the letter i on it. If you hear the /i/ sound, I want you to raise your card. If you don t here it, keep your card on your desk. 6. Have students get out their paper and pencil. "We are going to learn to make the letter i. Begin at the fence line and draw a straight line down. Then go between the fence line and the sky line and make a dot. When you get finished, show me. Then I want you to make a whole row of them. 7. For assessment, hand out picture page and get students to name each picture. Then get students to circle each picture whose names have /i/ in them. Eldredge, J. Loyd. (1995) Teaching Decoding in Holistic Classrooms Click here to return to Illuminations Momma I mean we just keepin' it. From one main idea, our brains tend to branch off to other ideas. Those are linked to still more ideas. A great way to learn something new is to create a link to something you already know. Here, a mind map can be helpful. How can your teen mind map? He'll need a pencil, an eraser and a sheet of blank paper. In the center of the paper, have him write down the most important word or short phrase of the subject he's learning. Circle it. Around the circle, write other important words. Use overlapping circles or arrows to connect items. At this point, your teen should work quickly. Don't analyze. The goal is to get as many ideas down on paper as possible. Now edit. He might move some items close to related ideas. Color can help organize ideas. Then he can continue working outward, adding new information to what he already knew. As the map expands, your teen will get more and more specific. When it is finished, your teen will have his personal map of what he already knew with what he has learned. People have different traditions in different parts of the world for when to wear footwear. For example, in many countries, usually do not wear their shoes or boots in a home. Clear Your URL History Using Your Registry in Windows Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of clearing your URL history using the Windows registry. The URL history is a list of websites that have been visited recently, which can be found in various applications such as Internet Explorer or Microsoft Edge. While this feature can be useful for quickly accessing frequently-visited sites, some users may prefer to clear their history for privacy reasons. This tutorial provides an alternative method to deleting individual entries from within specific applications, by editing the registry directly. Please note that modifying the registry carries certain risks, including potential system instability if changes are made incorrectly. Always ensure to create a backup before making any modifications to the registry. Additionally, these steps require administrative privileges; please log in with an account that has sufficient permissions. Step 1: Open the Run dialog box To begin, press the Windows key + R to open the Run dialog box. Alternatively, navigate to Start > Run or search for 'Run' in the Start menu. Step 2: Type regedit in the Run dialog box In the text field provided, type 'regedit' (without quotes) and click OK. If prompted by User Account Control, select Yes to grant permission to continue. Step 3: Navigate to the correct location in the Registry Editor The Registry Editor window should now appear. Expand HKEY_CURRENT_USER by clicking the arrow next to it. Then expand Software, then Microsoft, followed by Internet Explorer. Click on TypedURLs to reveal its contents. Note: Although we are using Internet Explorer's key here, this also affects other browsers like Microsoft Edge since they share similar components. Step 4: Select all keys under TypedURLs Press Ctrl + A to select all keys under TypedURLs. You'll see several numbered folders (keys). These represent different URLs stored in your browser's memory. Step 5: Delete selected keys Right-click on one of the highlighted keys and choose Delete. Confirm when asked whether you want to delete the item(s). Repeat this action until all keys are deleted. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Be cautious while performing these actions, especially during Step 5. Make sure only the intended items are selected before proceeding with deletion. * Creating a System Restore Point prior to changing anything in the registry would allow reverting back to a stable state if something goes wrong. * Remember, this procedure doesn't prevent future recording of visited URLs. It merely clears existing records. To disable further logging, consider adjusting settings within your web browser itself. * This method applies mainly to older versions of Windows OS and might not work identically across newer ones due to variations in UI design and functionality. However, equivalent paths exist even in modern systems where similar tasks could be accomplished via analogous procedures. where's the tire that Daddy bought to make a swing. The activities listed below provide links to other areas in the curriculum. - Draw pictures of different foods in each category and try to think of interesting foods. For example, for the fruit group, draw mango and kiwi. For the bread, cereal, rice, and pasta group, consider putting in barley, couscous, and bagels. - Encourage each student to work with an adult at home to develop a health log for 1 week. The log should include specific information about what the student eats each day, what he or she does for exercise, and what time he or she goes to sleep at night and wakes up each morning. At the end of the week, have each student bring his/her health log in and share it with the class. How are the logs different? How are they similar? What can students learn from reading the logs? - Bring in food labels from several common foods, such as cereal, crackers, cookies, and juice. As a class, discuss what information they include. What can students learn about the ingredients in the food? What can they learn about the nutrients the food contains? - Bring in a map and have each student point out where the food is from and why it is commonly eaten in that part of the world. If possible, have an international food festival, with students and their parents bringing in foods that students researched and discussed. - Have each child learn about a particular dance or exercise he or she can share with the class. Each of the students can teach the rest of the class one move from the dance or exercise. They can do this at home for more exercise. Most of the materials that are called "plastic" are polymers. Polymers are long chains of atoms bonded to each other. In most plastics, the long chain is a chain of carbon atoms with other atoms attached to them. The different atoms and the shape and length of the chains change how the plastic looks and works. Some might think that at absolute zero particles lose all energy and stop moving. This is not correct. In quantum physics there is something called zero point energy, which means that even after all the energy from particles has been removed, the particles still have some energy. This is due to Heisenberg's uncertainty principle, which states that the more that is known about a particle's position, the less that can be known about its momentum, and vice versa. Therefore, a particle cannot be completely stopped because then its exact position and momentum would be known. " There she is running up there. So I'd just like to ask you a few questions. You know what to do with an ice cream cone. A Crookes' radiometer has four vanes suspended inside a glass bulb. Inside the bulb, there is a good vacuum. When you shine a light on the vanes in the radiometer, they spin -- in bright sunlight, they can spin at several thousand rotations per minute! The vacuum is important to the radiometer's success. If there is no vacuum (that is, if the bulb is full of air), the vanes do not spin because there is too much drag. If there is a near-perfect vacuum, the vanes do not spin unless they are held in a frictionless way. If the vanes have a frictionless support and the vacuum is complete, then photons bouncing off the silver side of the vanes push the vanes, causing them to rotate. However, this force is exceedingly small. If there is a good but incomplete vacuum, then a different effect called thermal transpiration occurs along the edges of the vanes, as described on this page. The effect looks as though the light is pushing against the black faces. The black side of the vane moves away from the light. Title: How to Appear Offline to Specific Skype Users on PC or Mac Skype is a popular communication tool that allows users to make voice calls, video calls, and send instant messages. However, there might be situations where you want to appear offline to specific contacts while still being online for others. This tutorial will guide you through the process of achieving this on your PC or Mac. **Step 1: Open Skype Application** Start by launching the Skype application on either your Windows PC or macOS system. Make sure you're logged into your account with all your contacts visible. **Step 2: Navigate to Contacts List** Once you've successfully logged in, look at the left-hand side panel of the window. Here, you should see a section named 'Contacts'. Click on it to view all your Skype contacts. **Step 3: Identify the Contact(s) to Appear Offline To** Go through your list of contacts and identify those individuals whom you wish to appear offline to. It could be anyone - a friend, family member, coworker, etc. Remember, appearing offline doesn't mean blocking them entirely; they just won't know whether you're online or not. **Step 4: Right-Click on the Selected Contact** After identifying the contact(s), right-click on their name from the contacts list. A context menu will pop up providing various options related to that particular contact. **Step 5: Hover Over 'Privacy'* In the popped-up context menu, move your cursor over the option labeled as 'Privacy'. Doing so will bring down another sub-menu containing privacy settings specifically for that contact. **Step 6: Select 'Appear As Invisible'** From the dropped-down sub-menu under 'Privacy', click on 'Appear As Invisible'. By selecting this option, you'll be able to hide your online status from the chosen contact without affecting your availability to other people in your contacts list. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** - *You can reverse this setting anytime:* If you change your mind about hiding your online presence from someone, simply repeat the steps above but select 'Available' instead of 'Appear As Invisible.' - *This method works only when the selected contact isn't already added to your recent chats or group conversations.* If they are, they may still see you online within these chat windows even if you set yourself as invisible to them individually. - *It does not prevent received calls or messages:* Even though the selected contact sees you as offline, they can still call or message you. You would need to manually decline such calls or ignore messages. - *Check your overall visibility:* While you can control individual contact's perception of your online status, ensure you haven't accidentally set yourself globally invisible (File > Privacy > Options). Otherwise, no one will be able to see you as available regardless of any individual settings. Title: How to Become a Dual Enrollment Student Dual enrollment is a program that allows high school students to take college-level courses and earn both high school and college credit simultaneously. This can be an excellent opportunity for motivated students to challenge themselves academically, save time and money on their path to a degree, and get a head start on their college education. Here's a comprehensive guide on becoming a dual enrollment student. ### Step 1: Research Dual Enrollment Programs Before diving into the process, research various dual enrollment programs available in your area. Each institution may have different requirements, course offerings, and procedures. Consider factors such as: * The colleges or universities partnering with your high school. * Available fields of study and specific courses. * Transferability of credits to other institutions. * Cost per credit (some schools provide financial assistance). * Transportation logistics and schedules. Key tip: Reach out to academic advisors at your high school and potential partner colleges for more information about their respective programs. ### Step 2: Meet Eligibility Criteria Am I putting it in the right way? Lure. A figure of a fowl made of leather and feathers. Make. An old staunch hawk that sets an example to young ones. Mantling. Stretching first one wing and then the other over the legs. Mew. It looked so inviting, so he decided to climb back down to the lake. As soon as the boy got to the lake, he noticed a boat on the shore. He asked the boat, "Are you available?" Title: How to Handle a Mistake Introduction: Making mistakes is an inevitable part of life. It's essential to learn from them and handle them gracefully rather than letting them discourage or define you. This article will provide a comprehensive guide on handling mistakes effectively, ensuring that you maintain your self-confidence, rectify errors promptly, and prevent future occurrences. By following these steps, you can transform setbacks into opportunities for growth and improvement. Step 1: Acknowledge Your Mistake Recognize when you have made an error. Be honest with yourself and avoid making excuses or blaming others. Understand that acknowledging your mistake is crucial as it allows you to take responsibility for your actions and initiate corrective measures. This transparency fosters trust among colleagues, friends, and family members. Key Tip: Avoid dwelling on the mistake; instead, focus on addressing it constructively. Step 2: Stay Calm and Collected It's natural to feel upset or frustrated after realizing you've made a mistake. However, it's important to regulate your emotions before taking any further action. Take deep breaths, compose yourself, and remind yourself that everyone makes mistakes. Maintaining calmness enables clear thinking and prevents rash decisions that could exacerbate the situation. Guideline: Consider practicing mindfulness techniques such as meditation or yoga to improve emotional regulation skills. Step 3: Analyze the Situation Once composed, analyze the circumstances surrounding your mistake. Determine what led to the error, if there were any warning signs, and whether external factors played a role. Thoroughly understanding the context will help identify strategies to remedy the issue and reduce the likelihood of repeating it. Key Tip: Objectivity is critical during this stage. Try to view the situation dispassionately without assigning blame or judgment. Step 4: Develop a Solution Based on your analysis, devise a plan to address the consequences of your mistake. Depending on the severity and complexity, consult relevant individuals who may be impacted by or possess expertise related to the problem. Collaborative efforts often yield more effective solutions while demonstrating accountability and respect towards affected parties. Guideline: Prioritize communication and collaboration when seeking input from others. Clearly articulate the issue at hand and actively listen to their suggestions. Step 5: Implement the Solution Put your proposed solution into practice diligently. Monitor progress closely and make adjustments as necessary. If applicable, inform those affected by the mistake about the steps taken to resolve the matter. Being proactive in keeping stakeholders informed builds confidence in your ability to manage adversity competently. Key Tip: Document the process and outcomes meticulously. This record serves as evidence of your commitment to resolving the mistake and provides valuable insights for preventing similar issues in the future. Step 6: Learn From the Experience After successfully managing the fallout from your mistake, reflect on the experience to extract lessons learned. Identify specific actions you can take to minimize the chances of repeating the error. Share your insights with peers or mentors to foster open dialogue around learning from missteps. Guideline: Regularly review past mistakes as part of personal development initiatives. Reflecting on errors encourages continuous growth and reinforces adaptability. Conclusion: Handling a mistake requires humility, composure, analytical thinking, creativity, and introspection. Embracing these qualities empowers you to confront challenges head-on, emerge stronger, and nurture positive relationships built on trust and mutual respect. Remember, every mistake presents an opportunity for growth - seize it! Ready for your succulent lips to roll around it and invite it home! What's so great about the Auburn mall? Remember, The proportion x:y does not equal the fraction . For example, if the ratio of boys to girls in a classroom is 1:2, that does not mean half of the class is boys and the other half is girls. It means that of the class is boys and of the class is girls. (But that means that the number of boys is half the number of girls.) But remember that the fraction only applies to the sum of the two sides , and can be wrong if there are more than 2 things. For example, if there is a fruit basket that has 16 fruits, and the ratio of apples to oranges is 3:1, that doesn't always mean that there are 12 apples and 4 oranges . There could be 12 bananas in the fruit basket, which means that there are only 3 apples and one orange. Now get the crayon and let's color a picture for grandma! So we'd like you to write it. Title: Understanding Logarithms: The Magic of Numbers Hello young explorers! Today, we are going to delve into the exciting world of numbers and learn about something called "logarithms." Now, don't let that big word scare you - logarithms are just another way to understand the magic behind numbers! Imagine you have a magical magnifying glass that can show you how many times bigger or smaller a number is, compared to another number. This special magnifying glass is called a "logarithm," and its job is to tell us "how many times do you multiply a given number to get a specific target number?" Let's explore this with an example using our trusty friend, the number 10. Let's say you want to know how many times you need to multiply 2 by itself to reach 100. You might think, "Well, 2x50 equals 100, so it must take 50 twos to make 100!" However, that's where our magical logarithm magnifying glass comes in handy. It tells us that you actually need to multiply 2 SEVEN times (because 2 x 2 x 2 x 2 x 2 x 2 x 2 equals 128, which is close to 100) to get close to 100. So, the logarithm of 100, when using base 2, is approximately 7. Now, you might wonder, "Why would anyone even care about that?" Well, imagine you're trying to figure out how many grains of sand there are on a beach. Counting them one by one would take forever! Instead, scientists estimate the size of the beach and then calculate the volume of each grain of sand. By raising the number of grains per cubic inch to the power of the exponent representing the volume, they can quickly determine the total number of grains. That's the real-life superpower of logarithms! When dealing with powers of ten, like understanding why 10 to the third power is equal to 1000, our magical magnifying glass shows that you need three tens multiplied together to create 1000. In other words, the logarithm of 1000, when using base 10, is exactly 3. But remember, sometimes our magical magnifying glass might give us a fractional or decimal answer because numbers don't always cooperate and fit neatly into whole categories. Just like how we saw earlier with the number 100, the logarithm gave us an approximate value instead of a nice round number. Don't worry; that's completely normal! So now you know all about logarithms and their magical ability to reveal hidden secrets within numbers. Keep exploring, asking questions, and discovering the beauty of mathematics in your daily life. Happy math adventures, young explorers! what'd happen if you do it the other way? Do you think the cat's gonna escape? XXX you would n't eat food this morning? She watched the bee and soon the boredom melted away. She smiled. The bee helped the little girl to feel happy again. She thanked it for making her day better and waved goodbye as it flew away. This paper was known as "Cosmological Considerations of the General Theory of Relativity" which made physicists start to change the assumption that the Universe never changed. A clause is a part of a sentence. Each clause has only one main verb. I love you is a sentence which has only one clause. I love you and I will always love you is a sentence which has two clauses. The two clauses are I love you and I will always love you. These clauses are joined together by the word and (a conjunction). Two clauses can be joined with a pronoun. For example: I live in London, which is in England. Here, I live in London is the first clause, and which is in England is the second clause. The word which is a pronoun which takes the place of London. It joins the two clauses. A sentence can contain many clauses. But sentences with fewer clauses are easier to understand. A subordinating clause is one which is dependent on the main clause (the primary clause). The main clause makes sense on its own. However, the subordinating clause does not. E.g. I love you makes perfect sense left on its own. However, and always will, does not. Puzzle of the Day Start with any two-digit number. Multiply each digit by itself then add these answers. Do the same thing to this new number and so on until something happens. For example 32 becomes 9+4 which is 13, 13 becomes 1+9 which is 10, 10 becomes 1+0 =1. This one ends at 1. What else happens when you start with different numbers? Answer: All end with single digits, 2 is most frequent If he thinks that he doesn't see their wheels then they're not moving. Title: Adding a Second Set of Data to an Excel Graph Step 1: Prepare Your Primary Dataset Before adding a second set of data to your graph, make sure you have already created a primary dataset that consists of at least two columns - one column for labels (categories or groups) and another column for corresponding numerical values. These will form the foundation of your first data series in the graph. Properly organizing this initial data is crucial as it sets up the structure needed for incorporating additional data points later. Key Tip: Use clear and concise label names that accurately represent their respective numeric values. This makes understanding the visual representation easier when interpreting the final graph. Step 2: Create a Basic Chart Based on the First Dataset Highlight both columns containing your labeled categories and numerical values by clicking and dragging over the cells. Then navigate to the 'Insert' tab within Microsoft Excel and select any chart type from the available options such as Column, Line, Area, Scatter etc., depending upon what kind of visualization suits best for presenting your information. After selecting, a basic chart should appear with the highlighted data integrated into it. Guideline: Choosing the right chart type depends on the nature of your data and story you want to tell through visualization. For example, line charts are great for time-series data while bar charts can effectively compare quantities across different categories. Step 3: Highlight the Secondary Dataset Now prepare your secondary dataset which also contains two similar columns like the primary dataset. Select these new rows including headers but excluding the original ones so only the newly added data gets highlighted. Be careful not to include any other unrelated data since it might skew the results or create confusion during interpretation. Step 4: Insert Another Series into the Same Chart Once the correct range has been selected, look under the 'Chart Tools > Design' tab where there should be an option called 'Select Data'. Clicking on this button opens a dialog box titled 'Data Range'. Within this popup, locate the section named 'Legend Entries (Series)' and click on 'Add'. Step 5: Specify Location of New Data Series Upon clicking 'Add', another dialog box appears asking for the 'Edit Series'. Here, specify the location of the new data series by entering the cell references manually in the format =SheetName!CellRange or simply using the mouse to highlight the required area. Make sure that all necessary cells are covered before confirming. Step 6: Verify Displayed Values Against Original Source After successfully inserting the second set of data, ensure that the displayed values match those present in the raw data source. Crosscheck every single entry if possible, especially focusing on areas showing significant differences between the two datasets. Mistakes could lead to misinterpretation; therefore accuracy is paramount. Key Tips: If after following these steps, some elements seem off, double check whether the proper ranges were chosen initially. Sometimes incorrect selection leads to unexpected outcomes. Also verify if the same unit measure applies to both datasets. Following these six steps meticulously allows users to add a second set of data seamlessly onto an existing excel graph. Not only does this enhance comprehension, but it provides valuable insights by visually comparing trends, patterns, correlations among various factors presented within the datasets. Grade Level or Age Group: I have done this with students grades 10-12, but it can no doubt be adapted for younger students. Lesson Objectives: The students will be invited to read Scriptures and to react to it in writing. - One Bible or Scripture Handout for each student. - Copies of the Scripture Journal Sheet - A scripture passage. There are several ways you might want to proceed. Below are a few suggestions: 1. Gospel reading a. Students decide which Gospel they will read. Tell them to start from the beginning of the Gospel they selected and to read through it. I suggest that you set a definite amount of time for them to read each day and that this be rather short. I have done this with senior students and asked them to read for 10-15 minutes at a time. This seemed to be just right for them. You might want to adjust that for younger students. b. Insist that this be a silent reading time. I have found that this was not a problem. I was amazed at how quickly the students settled down to read and how quiet the classroom became for almost the full allotted time. c. Once time is up, remind the students that it is time to write in their journal. You can have them follow the format suggested in the Scripture Journal Sheet or simply have them write a paragraph in which they react to what they have read. Tell them they have 5 minutes to do this. I have found that some of the students become restless if you extend the writing period too much beyond that. d. You can have your students do this only when you see them in class, or have them do it as an assignment at home. e. I have done this exercise in my Religious Ed classes 3 times a week for 3-4 weeks in a row (you will have to determine yourself by the way the students are responding how long to do it with your group). f. You might wish to pick up the students' journals at least once to read them. If you do, tell the students ahead of time (before they make their first entry) that you will be doing this. I always told students that I would only read what they wanted me to read and that they could simply write "don't read" beside any section they wanted me to skip. a. Select one or more of the readings of the day (U.S. Liturgical Calendar) . If you are in Canada, consult the CCCB web site here for the readings of the day since these may vary slightly from those in the U.S. b. Read the selected scripture passage(s) to the students or have one of them do so. c. Hand out a copy of the same scripture passage(s) or help the students find it in their Bible. d. Ask the students to quietly read the passage slowly and to do so at least twice. Decide ahead of time, depending on the length of the readings, how long the students will have to read. They should know this before they start reading. e. When the reading time is up, ask the students to write in their Scripture Journal. f. Insist that steps d & e above be done very quietly. Civil law is the legal system used in most countries around the world today. Civil law is based on legislation that is found in constitutions or statutes passed by government. The secondary part of civil law is the legal approaches that are part of custom. In civil law governments, judges do not generally have much power, and most of the laws and legal precedent are created by Members of Parliament. Once upon a time there was a girl and a boy. [takes a crayon out of the container and puts it in front of MOT] Object-oriented programming: a style of computer programming that uses different modules of code that build on each other or work together to create a program. OOP (for short) is commonly used today rather than line after line of code, to make programs more organized as collections of smaller units. This is an essential technique in programming today, and I will teach it to my best ability in this tutorial using the well-known programming language (and my favorite) Java. Note: this tutorial is currently a rough draft, but will be improved soon! Good luck! Step #1: Next - Before you begin coding, you need something called the JDK. This stands for Java Development Kit, and is Java's free way of letting you program in their language. Just click this link, and you will be led to the JDK 5.0 download page. - After downloading the JDK package, follow the instructions to install it on your computer. Make sure to use the default options for where you install the JDK, because that will come in handy later. Step #2: Next - Setup, part 2: - Next, to make programming in Java easier, download and install NetBeans. NetBeans is an Integrated Development Environment, or IDE for short, that was created just for Java. NetBeans can be downloaded here. I'd recommend that you install the Java SE package if you're new to Java. Step #3: Next - Setup, part 3: - Simply open the NetBeans installer and walk through the process. It will ask you where to install NetBeans and where the JDK was installed. If you used the default option for this in the JDK installer, you should be fine accepting the default option here. Check the checkbox that instructs the installer to open NetBeans when you're done and click "Finish." The installer will close, NetBeans will open and we can get started! Step #4: Next - Beginning your first project in NetBeans: - To start, go to File -> New -> Project. A window will open. Click "Next", and give your project a name (I will use "OOPExample1"). Once that's done, click "Finish." A file ("OOPExample1.java", if you used that name) will appear. Step #5: Next - Now we decide what will happen in our program. For this project, I will teach you how to make the game RPS (rock, paper, scissors): a simplistic yet fun game, great to learn to program. I will try to keep this tutorial simple to follow, and will explain the code as best I can. Step #6: Next - The following code may not make much sense if you are new to Java, but it will after I explain. The code has been attached to this project and can be accessed as RPSCode.pdf in the "Files" section. Open it, delete everything in your "OOPExample1.java" file, and paste this code inside. Now, hit the "Run" button (it looks like a "Play" button on a DVD player, green), and some text will appear. You can now play Rock, Paper, Scissors with your computer! To play again, hit the "Run" button again. Step #7: Next - Explanation of the Code: - We made it through the programming! Next is to understand it. Look back at your code. - The first line just tells what "package" this piece of code is in. This simply tells where your code is stored. The next line is an import statement: it lets you use extra commands so that you can do more stuff with your program. Scanner (what it imports) is a module that reads what you type in when the program asks for your move. - public class OOPExample1 just starts a new class (a collection of functions and variables). Inside there is a method (function): public static void main(String args). This is the main method, which runs when the program runs. - Inside is a bunch of code, the first of which is the line CPU cpu1 = new CPU();. This may look confusing, but all it does is create an instance (kind of like a personal copy) of the CPU class, which comes later. It also makes an instance of a Scanner: all that does is read what you type. These instances can be called by their names (cpu1 and sc1, respectively). - Next, we define (make) two variables: validmove (type boolean, either true or false) and userMove (a String (bunch of text) that stores what the user's move is). - The code inside the loop prints the message instructing you to type your move, (the System.out.println command), reads what you type (the nextLine command), converts it to lowercase (the toLowerCase) command, and tests it to see if the input is either "rock", "paper", or "scissors" (the if command; || means "or"). If one of those is true, validmove is set to true, and the loop ends. Phew! Now we have the user's move! - After the while loop, there is a command that defines another string: CPUMove. It's kind of self-explanatory what this string does: it stores the CPU's move. This line uses a method (function) called getCPUMove in the CPU Class (once again, a collection of variables and methods) to decide (randomly) what the computer's move should be. Finally, CPU's comparemoves method is called and it is given the strings CPUMove and userMove to let it know what moves have been made by the players. Step #8: Next - Explanation of the Code, part 2: - (Note: don't rely on this image for the code, because I could not fit it all into one image). Now I will explain the CPU class! By the way, if you have any questions about what any of this terminology is, there is a great tutorial here about understanding OOP, created by the makers of Java. - The first method is called comparemoves. Guess what it does? (Compares moves!). Inside are a bunch of if statements. Some of these are inside of each other, which might seem a little weird. Basically, all it does is see if the user played rock, paper, or scissors. Once it finds out which one he/she played, a bunch of if statements inside that find out what the CPU played and tell a different method, outputmoves, what to print on the screen. - Note that this method is called public void comparemoves. Void means that when it's called, it doesn't return anything. Returning a value means that the function passes back a value to the instruction that called it. The line String CPUMove = cpu1.getCPUMove(); takes what was returned from the method getCPUMove in cpu1 and puts it in the string CPUMove. Make sense? - Now let's talk about the outputmoves method, which the comparemoves method calls. All it does is put together a string, output, and print it to the console, where you typed your moves earlier. The System.out.println statement is pretty simple, as all it does is print the string output. The String output = ... statement might be a bit confusing, however. It seems pretty normal, but what's this concat() thing? It's a method that concatentates (fancy word for "pins onto the end") the thing in parentheses to the string before it. - This helps us put together the sentence "I played <move that the computer played>. <sentence that says who won>!" using the parameters that were passed to it (things that were given to it to work with). - Finally, we reach the getCPUMove method. This method randomly chooses what move the computer plays. The first line chooses a random number from just above 0 to just below 3. Then, the number is floored, or the decimal part is removed, making it a random integer from 0 to 2. Finally, it is cast (changed) to an integer type (it wasn't completely an integer before because of the fractional part), and is stored into choice. Next, a string rpsmove is created and set to nothing (""). Finally, there is a switch statement, which is a variant of the if statement. - All that it says is if choice equals 0, then rpsmove = "rock". If it equals 1, it is set to "paper", and if it is none of those (it must be 2), it is set to "scissors". Then it returns the move in the string rpsmove. Voil ! We've returned a random move from the computer! But before I finish, I'd like to point out some things about the switch statement. Notice that the variable being tested is inside the parentheses next to the word switch, and that there are break statements after each case. All these do is clear the path for the next case. OK! We're done! - Thank you for reading this! It must have taken a great effort to read. It took me a great one to write. I hope that this project got you interested in Java programming (if it didn't bore you to death with all of the explanation), and that you will continue to explore this beautiful language. For a link to the beginning of a complete tutorial on Java, click here. - Good luck! Once upon a time, there was a polite little boy named Tim. The Sphinx looked at him angrily. "The Owl is a conceited boaster!" he cried excitedly. "Excuse me!" stammered the Sparrow, frightened. Welcome, Grade-School Students! Today, let's learn about something fascinating called Pi (\ ), which has to do with circles and patterns in numbers. You might have heard about it before or seen it in your math class when learning about circles. But did you know that computers can also work with this special number, and we can study its computational complexity? Let's dive into understanding what all these words mean! Imagine writing down the digits of Pi, just like you write down your phone number - 3.14159265... and so on. Now, imagine asking questions like: "What is the 100th digit of Pi?" or "Does the sequence '123' appear anywhere in the first million digits of Pi?" These are some of the things researchers explore when studying Pi's computational complexity. First, think about guessing whether the 100th digit of Pi is a 1 or 0. This task isn't too difficult because Pi doesn't change quickly between its digits. So, you could check by calculating Pi up to the 100th digit. That means you don't need a lot of space to remember many earlier digits while checking the 100th digit. Researchers call this concept PSPACE, meaning it uses polynomial space - just enough room needed for the job, but no more than necessary. But here comes another question: Can we find out if a particular sequence appears somewhere within the first billion digits of Pi without having to calculate all those billions of digits? Surprisingly, yes! By using smart shortcuts, researchers discovered ways to search through large chunks of Pi efficiently. They found patterns allowing them to predict where certain sequences would show up. This achievement places Pi's computational complexity in NP^L, which means it's connected to non-deterministic polynomial time, a fancy term describing a problem solver who makes lucky guesses sometimes. So, why does all this matter? Well, discoveries in fields like computational complexity help us understand how our world works, especially when it involves numbers and calculations. Just like knowing how to sort toys in different boxes helps you keep your playroom organized, grasping concepts in mathematics enables us to make sense of complex ideas around us. Who knows-perhaps someday YOU will contribute to uncovering new secrets hiding in Pi's infinite string of digits! I don't know how to spell it. - Locate the longest continuous chain of carbon atoms. Remember that the longest chain may be bent. - Number your chain from the end nearest a double or triple bond, otherwise from the end with the closest branch. - Use Greek numerical prefixes to find the "parent name" of your compound. This is based on the size of your longest chain. For example, if you have a C6 (6-carbon chain) molecule, it is considered a hexane. C7 is heptane, C8 is octane, and so on. These are all alkanes (single bonds only). Alkenes: If the chain contains a double bond end the name with -ene. For example, a C9 containing a double bond is a nonene. Place the number of the adjacent carbon in front of the name. Ex: CH2=CHCH2CH3 is but-1-ene. OR Simply butene, because, generally 1 is ommited . Alkynes: If the chain contains a triple bond end the name with -yne. For example a C5 containing a triple bond is a pentyne. Multiple double bonds take the form -diene, -triene, etc., with the size prefix of the chain taking an extra "a". Alkynes are named using the same system, with the suffix "-yne" indicating a triple bond. Ex: CH2=CHCH=CH2 is 1,3-butadiene. - Name your side chains. They are named for the carbon on the main chain to which they are attached. For example, if you have a C1 compound attached to the second carbon in the chain, it is called a 1-methyl group (use the same prefixes as above). If there is more than one of the same group, add the number of the carbon of attachment to the name. Using the previous example with the addition of a methane on the the third carbon as well, you would name it a 1,3-dimethyl group. Prefixes for groups: - Arrange your side chains in alphabetical order and tack them on before your parent name. Complex side chains go in parentheses. - Keep practicing! Before you know it, you'll be able to name or draw a 1,2-di(1,2 dimethyl ethyl) cyclopentane with ease! Watch out for rings. They are named for the number of carbons in the central ring; for example, a 5-carbon ring is a cyclopentane. C1 through C4 compounds do not follow the prefix naming rule. C1 is methane, C2 is ethane, C3 is propane, and C4 is butane. Many compounds are called by their common names instead of using the new IUPAC system. For example, a side chain that would be named under the IUPAC system as (1-methylethyl) is also known as an isopropyl group. Be careful not to mix your naming systems. 12 April 2011Comment Ader, Cohen and Felten went on to edit the groundbreaking book "Psychoneuroimmunology" in 1981, which laid out the underlying premise that the brain and immune system represent a single, integrated system of defense. However, if you've decided that Tinder Plus no longer meets your needs or falls outside your budget, cancelling your subscription is a straightforward process. This tutorial will guide you through canceling your Tinder Plus subscription on your iPhone or iPad. **Key Tips:** 1. Make sure you have a stable internet connection throughout the cancellation process. 2. Be aware of your renewal date to avoid being charged for an extra month. 3. Double-check that you are signed in with the correct Apple ID associated with your Tinder Plus subscription. **Step 1: Open Settings App** On your home screen, find and tap on the gray gear icon labeled "Settings." The Settings app allows you to manage various aspects of your device, including subscriptions. **Step 2: Navigate to Your Account** Scroll down until you see your name and profile picture at the top of the menu. Tap on this section to access your account details. **Step 3: Select iTunes & App Store** Under your account information, locate and select "iTunes & App Store" from the list. This option will take you to the page where you can view and manage your active subscriptions. **Step 4: View Apple ID Information** At the top of the iTunes & App Store settings page, there should be your email address followed by "[...]" (e.g., "[your.email@example.com](mailto:your.email@example.com)"). Tap on this line to open a dropdown menu. From here, choose "View Apple ID" and authenticate yourself using Face ID, Touch ID, or password when prompted. **Step 5: Access Subscriptions** After successfully authenticating, scroll down until you reach the "Subscriptions" header. Underneath this header, click on "Manage" to proceed. You may now view all of your current subscriptions managed via your Apple ID, including your Tinder Plus membership. **Step 6: Locate and Select Tinder Plus** From the list of subscriptions, look for "Tinder Plus" and single-tap it to reveal more options regarding your subscription management. **Step 7: Cancel Subscription** Once you are on the Tinder Plus info page, find the "Active" label next to your subscription details. To the right side of the "Active" label, there should be a small button resembling a clock with a horizontal line; tap on it. A popup window will appear asking whether you would like to confirm your decision to cancel the subscription. Choose "Confirm" to finalize the cancellation request. **Step 8: Verify Renewal Status** To ensure your cancellation was successful, return to the Tinder Plus info page and check under the "Active" label again. It should indicate that your subscription has been cancelled and display the expiration date. By following these steps, you have effectively cancelled your Tinder Plus subscription on your iPhone or iPad. Remember that uninstalling the Tinder app alone does not automatically terminate any ongoing subscriptions. Cornelia their mother left Rome and went to live at Misenum. Title: How to Catch Scammers on Roblox Adopt Me Introduction: Roblox's Adopt Me is an incredibly popular game with millions of active players worldwide. Unfortunately, this popularity also attracts scammers looking to take advantage of unsuspecting users. This guide will teach you how to identify, avoid, and report these scams to ensure your safety while playing Adopt Me. Step 1: Familiarize Yourself With Common Scams Before attempting to catch scammers, learn about common scam types within Adopt Me. These include: - Trade scams (offering fake or overpriced items) - Giveaway scams (promising free rare pets or other valuable items) - Phishing scams (asking for personal information like passwords or usernames) Becoming familiar with these tactics can help you recognize them when they occur. Key Tip: Always be skeptical of unbelievable offers; if something seems too good to be true, it probably is! Step 2: Verify User Reputation When engaging in trades or giveaways, check the reputation of those involved by searching their username on platforms such as ROBLOX Forums or social media groups dedicated to Adopt Me. Users with positive feedback from multiple sources are less likely to be scammers. Guideline: Avoid interacting with new accounts without any history or verifiable reputation. Step 3: Check Trading Values Beforehand To prevent falling victim to trade scams, consult reputable websites that provide up-to-date values for Adopt Me items, ensuring both parties receive fair deals. Examples include adoptme.net and robloxadoptvalues.com. Key Tip: Never trust self-proclaimed 'value experts' who approach you randomly claiming certain items have higher value than generally accepted. Step 4: Watch Out for Impersonators Scammers may impersonate trusted community members, moderators, or developers to gain credibility. To avoid being tricked, verify identities through known channels like official Discord servers or verified social media profiles before proceeding further. Guideline: Be cautious when receiving friend requests or messages from unknown users claiming to represent authority figures. Step 5: Keep Personal Information Private Never share sensitive data like account credentials, real name, address, phone number, or email addresses with anyone, even if they claim to work for ROBLOX or Adopt Me staff. Legitimate representatives never require this type of information. Key Tip: Use unique, complex passwords for all online gaming accounts and consider using two-factor authentication whenever possible. Step 6: Report Suspicious Activity Immediately If you encounter potential scams, report them immediately via the built-in reporting system within Adopt Me or directly contact ROBLOX Support. By doing so, you contribute to maintaining a safe environment for everyone. Guideline: Provide detailed descriptions, screenshots, and chat logs when submitting reports to assist investigations effectively. Step 7: Educate Others About Scams Share your knowledge about various scamming techniques with friends, family members, and fellow gamers. Awareness reduces vulnerability and strengthens the entire player base against fraudulent activities. Conclusion: Stay vigilant, informed, and proactive in protecting yourself and others from scammers in Adopt Me. Remember that genuine opportunities usually come from reliable sources, whereas suspicious interactions should be treated with caution. Happy gaming! A decimal is a number that can be written as a fraction whose denominator is 1, 10, 100, 1000, and so on. For example: Decimal Notation = Fraction Notation In a whole number, the decimal point is located to the right of the digit in the ones place. For example: (252 = 252 .). The place value of a digit is determined by where it is in relation to the decimal point. The digits to the left of the decimal point are whole numbers; they have place values of 1, 10, 100, 1000, 10,000 and so on. The digits to the right of the decimal point are fractional parts; they have place values of and so on. Note: each place is as large as the place to its immediate In the place value chart, the numbers to the left of the decimal point end in s and represent the whole number part of the decimal while the numbers to the right of the decimal point end in ths and represent the fractional part of To write the word name for a Decimal: If there is no number other than 0 to the left of the decimal point, omit steps one and two. - Write the name for the whole number to the left of the - Write the word and for the decimal point. - Write the name for the number to the right of the decimal point as if it were a whole number. Then write the name for the place value of the last digit on the right. Example: 253.5674 is two hundred fifty-three and five thousand six hundred seventy-four ten- thousandths. To add or subtract decimals, line up all the decimal points in a vertical column. Example 1. add: 10.5 + 3 +.072 + 195.0035 Example 2. Subtract: 123.7450 2.00034 To multiply two decimals: - Multiply the two numbers as if they were whole numbers. - Locate the decimal point by counting the number of decimal places (to the right of the decimal point) in both numbers. The total of these two counts is the number of decimal places the product must have. - If necessary, add zeros to the left of the numeral so that there are enough decimal places. 1. 2.7 x 4 = 10.8 Notice that there is 1 decimal place in the product. 2. 3.456 x .5 = 1.7280 In this product, there are 4 decimal places. 1.7280 can also be written as 3. .45 x .12 Notice that 45 x 12 = 540, but there should be 4 decimal places in the product. Therefore, add a zero to the left of the 5. .45 x .12 = .0540 = .054 To divide two decimal numbers: - If the divisor is not a whole number, move both decimal points to the right the same number of decimal places until the divisor is a whole number. - Place the decimal point in the quotient above the decimal place in the dividend. - Divide as if both numbers were whole numbers. - If the numbers do not divide evenly, round off to the given place value. Multiplication and Division by powers of Ten: A power of ten is a number that can be written as a product of tens; 10, 100, 1000, 10000 ..are powers of ten. In exponential form, these are A power of ten can be recognized by looking for the number ten written with an exponent or a single 1 followed by zeros. To multiply a number by a power of ten, move the decimal point to the right. To divide a number by a power of ten, move the decimal point to the left. The number of places to move is shown by the number of zeros in the power of ten. If the exponent of ten is negative, move the decimal point to the left as in Enable Indicator Lights for Open Applications in Mac OS X Lion ========================================================== Follow this detailed guide to enable indicator lights for open applications in Mac OS X Lion. This can be particularly useful if you have multiple displays or workspaces and want a visual cue for which applications are currently running. ### Step 1: Access System Preferences Click on the Apple icon located at the top left corner of your screen. From the drop-down menu, select "System Preferences." ### Step 2: Go to Universal Access In the System Preferences window, click on the "Universal Access" icon, which is typically found in the third row of options. ### Step 3: Select Seeing Tab Once the Universal Access window opens, click on the "Seeing" tab located at the top of the window. ### Step 4: Check the 'Flash the LED for alerts' Option Under the Seeing tab, you will find an option called "Flash the LED for alerts". Check the box next to it to enable indicator lights for open applications. Key Tips & Guidelines --------------------- * The LED mentioned here refers to the small light embedded into the front edge of your MacBook Pro (unibody) or iMac's camera lens. It usually glows green when activated. * If you don't see any change after enabling this feature, ensure that your computer isn't muted and that you have allowed some time for the system to recognize the new setting. * This feature does not affect the functionality of your webcam; it only uses the status light associated with it. * To test if it's working correctly, try opening an application like Mail or Safari and observe whether the LED starts flashing. Additional Steps for Notification Center Settings (Optional) ----------------------------------------------------------- If you are using macOS Sierra or later versions instead of Mac OS X Lion, you might need to adjust settings related to Notifications because they handle alert sounds differently. Here's how: ### Step A: Open Notifications preferences Click on the Apple icon > System Preferences > Notifications. ### Step B: Adjust Sound Settings Select an app from the list on the left side, then make sure 'Play sound for notifications' is checked under its options on the right side. Repeat this process for all necessary apps. Conclusion ---------- Enabling indicator lights for open applications in Mac OS X Lion provides a simple yet effective way to keep track of active programs. By following these clear and practical steps, users can enhance their productivity and multitask more efficiently. Remember, though, that this feature may vary slightly depending on your specific model of Mac and the version of the operating system you are using. Mommy that's de airplane day wouldn't go in. Disable Raise to Listen for Audio Messages on an iPhone: A Comprehensive Guide The Apple iPhone offers a convenient feature that allows users to listen to audio messages by raising the phone to their ear, similar to making a call. This feature, known as "Raise to Listen," can be helpful but may also cause accidental actions or unwanted interruptions. If you wish to disable this feature, follow these steps carefully. We've included detailed explanations, key tips, and guidelines to ensure clarity and ease of application. Step 1: Unlock your iPhone Ensure your device is unlocked and you have access to the home screen. This step might seem obvious, but starting with a secure and accessible device guarantees a smooth process without any hindrances. Step 2: Open the Settings app Locate the gray gear icon labeled 'Settings.' Tap on it to open the settings menu. The Settings app contains various customization options for your iPhone, including disabling the Raise to Listen feature. Step 3: Access the Messages section In the Settings app, scroll down until you find the 'Messages' option (it should appear above 'Mobile Data'). Selecting this will take you to the message-specific settings where we can turn off the Raise to Listen function. Step 4: Locate the Audio Messages setting Within the Messages settings, look for 'Audio Messages,' which should be located near the bottom of the list under 'Send & Receive' and 'Group Messaging.' Tapping on this option opens another set of configurations related explicitly to audio messaging. Step 5: Adjust Expire settings (optional) Before proceeding to disable Raise to Listen, consider changing the expiration duration of your audio messages from 'After 2 Minutes' to 'Never.' By doing so, your audio messages remain available unless manually deleted, ensuring no important information gets lost unintentionally. Although optional, adjusting the expiration setting complements deactivating the Raise to Listen feature since both aim at preventing accidental interactions. Step 6: Return to Audio Message settings Once satisfied with the Expire settings, navigate back to the main Audio Messages page. You should now see two slider buttons next to 'Raise To Listen' and 'Speak Auto-play Message.' Step 7: Disable Raise To Listen To turn off the Raise to Listen functionality, locate the button beside 'Raise To Listen.' It should initially be green and positioned towards the right side, indicating that the feature is currently active. Tap on this button once; its color should change to white or grey, signifying that Raise to Listen has been successfully disabled. Key Tips and Guidelines * Make sure you are familiar with other methods of playing audio messages, such as tapping the play button within the Messages app or using Siri voice commands, before turning off Raise to Listen. * Remember that after disabling Raise to Listen, you must tap the audio message to hear it. Be mindful when handling sensitive content like personal conversations or confidential work discussions. * Periodically review your Audio Messages settings to maintain optimal usage based on preferences and updated iOS features. * Always double-check whether the Raise to Listen toggle is enabled or disabled whenever experiencing unexpected behavior while listening to audio messages. Title: How to Draw the Egyptian Pyramids Drawing the majestic Egyptian pyramids can be an enjoyable way to express your creativity while learning about ancient architecture. This step-by-step guide will provide clear instructions and helpful tips so that you can draw accurate representations of these iconic structures. **Things You'll Need:** 1. Paper (preferably with grid lines) 2. Pencil or pen 3. Eraser (optional) 4. Ruler (optional) 5. Coloring materials (markers, colored pencils, etc.) **Step 1: Understand the Basics of Pyramid Structure** Before starting to draw, familiarize yourself with the basic structure of a pyramid. An Egyptian pyramid typically has four triangular faces meeting at a point called the apex, along with a square base. The sides are aligned with the cardinal directions - north, south, east, and west. ![Pyramid Diagram](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/a/ad/Draw-an-Egyptian-Pyramid-Step-1-Version-3.jpg/aid67890-v4- Lake Balaton, Central Europe's largest lake can be found in Hungary. (En espa ol: Hematoma) This word looks a little like tomato, but you don't grow hematomas in the garden! Hematoma is just a medical word for bruise, or black and blue mark. The "hema" part of the word means blood, which is what you see under your skin if you get a bad bump. When you get a bad bump, the blood vessels break and the blood leaks out. But then your body goes to work to clean things up. Before you know it, the black and blue color has faded to green or yellow and then it's all gone. By: Dee Globetti Growing Independence & Fluency Lesson Plan Rationale: To become fluent readers, children need to learn how to read faster, smoother, and more expressively. Fluency refers to a student's ability to read words accurately and automatically. In this lesson, students will learn how to read quickly, smoothly, and expressively in order to gain fluency. Students will gain fluency through repeated readings, timed readings, and one-minute reads. It has been proven that the more students read the more their reading skills will improve. Class copies and Teacher copy of decodable book: Let's Go Froggy Individual posters for class with clouds and numbers graphed on it for each child to place kites on to for tracking fluency progress 3 small kites for each student (different colors) with names on them (for their poster) Stopwatch for each student Class bulletin board covered in clouds to place each child's poster onto for display of achievement 1."First, we are going to review the strategies we can use when we don't recognize a word. If we come to the letters f r o g but cannot read the word, first we look at the vowel sound. In this word, o = /o/. Next, we go to the beginning sound. f = /f/. The next sound is r-r-r like a railroad. If we add the vowel sound we have fff-rrr-ooo. Finally, we look at the last sound. It is g = /g/. Now we can put all four sounds together to read stretching it out: fffrrroooggg. Frog! Great job! Now, when we come to words we don't know when we are reading, this vowel-first method is a great strategy to use when you can't figure out the word." 2."When we read, we need to make sure that we read smoothly just as if we are talking so that we can understand what we read. I am going to read a sentence to the class without reading smoothly, or what we call fluently: T-o-d-ay w-e ar-e g-o-ing t-o p-r-a-c-t-i-ce r-ea-d-ing s-m-oo-th-l-y. Is this easy to understand if I were to read a whole book to you like that? If I read the sentence with fluency, 'Today we are going to practice reading smoothly,' it sounds better and makes more sense because the words are said all together and read clearly. The first time I read the sentence each word was broken up into each sound I heard in the word, but the second time I read the sentence it improved because I put all the sounds together to make words to read the sentence all together. Since this is how we all need to read, and want our goal to be able to read fluently, we have to keep practicing." (Pass out books for each reading group.) Give booktalk: " Spring is here, and Froggy can't wait to go on a bike trip with his dad. But he needs to get everything he needs to ride before sunset. Will he make it?" 3."Each person in your group is going to practice reading through the text three times. Our goal is to read 60 words in one minute. As one of you is reading, the other needs to be timing him or her on the stopwatch and stopping the reader at 60 seconds. After the 60 seconds is up, go back and count how many words you read in the minute and then write it down. Do this three times each, and when you are down we are going to use our kites to show how we improved every time we read on a chart. When you are done, raise your hand and I will help you graph your results on your chart." 4."I want everyone to practice as much as you can, because the more you practice, the faster you get, and the more you will be able to read and understand. You can take the books home and practice your reading with your parents or friends, or do it during DEAR (drop everything and read) time with a friend. Remember what we practiced today in class when we got stuck on a word while reading, and how to read the sentences smoothly." Assessment: Students may be assessed for fluency by one minute reads. The students will also use a fluency chart to keep up with their progress by moving their kite on the poster for the highest WPM after three one minute reads. After a book is read three or four times, a new book should be introduced and one minute reads should be repeated three or four more times. Bracken, Rebecca. Flying Away with Fluency http://www.auburn.edu/rdggenie/explor/brackengf.html London, Jonathon. Let's Go Froggy. Picture Puffin, 1994. Title: How to Craft a Fence in Minecraft Minecraft is a popular sandbox game that allows players to build and explore their own worlds. One of the many items you can craft in this game is a fence. Fences are essential for keeping mobs out of your base or enclosing animals in pens. This tutorial will guide you through the process of crafting a fence in Minecraft. **Step 1: Gather Materials** To craft a fence, you need sticks and wooden planks. You can obtain these materials by chopping down trees with an axe. Each log dropped from a tree can be turned into four wooden planks using a crafting table. To create sticks, place two wooden planks above each other in the crafting interface. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Any type of wood (oak, spruce, birch, jungle, acacia, dark oak) can be used to make fences. - Make sure you have enough space in your inventory to store the collected logs, planks, and sticks. **Step 2: Open Your Crafting Table** Right-click on your crafting table in the game world to open the 3x3 crafting grid. The crafting table provides more slots than the standard player's inventory, enabling you to craft more complex items like fences. Key Tips & Guidelines: - If you don't have a crafting table yet, first create one by placing wooden planks in the survival inventory's crafting area (the middle box). **Step 3: Arrange Items in the Crafting Grid** Place the sticks and wooden planks diagonally adjacent to each other in the crafting grid as follows: one stick in the top left corner, one wooden plank directly below it, another stick in the bottom right corner, and another wooden plank directly above it. The pattern should resemble a backwards 'L'. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Be careful about the arrangement of items; changing the order may result in different items being crafted. - Always ensure that the correct sequence is followed - sticks first, then wooden planks diagonally. **Step 4: Collect Your Fences** Once you've arranged the sticks and wooden planks correctly, the fences will appear in the result box of the crafting table window. Drag them into your inventory. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Only fully completed recipes yield results. So if your fence isn't appearing, double check the placement of your items. **Step 5: Place Your Fences** With the fences now stored in your inventory, position your cursor over the spot where you want to place them. A small square icon representing the placed block will appear along with its name ('Fence'). Left click to lay down the fence block. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Unlike most blocks, fences can be placed without support underneath. However, they do connect to other fences, walls, and gates when adjacent. By following these steps, you'll have successfully crafted and placed a fence in Minecraft! Remember, practice makes perfect, so feel free to experiment further with various arrangements until you get comfortable with the mechanics. "Oh, very, very. And I know mamma distresses over it. A few words which she let fall this morning, betrayed it plainly. I am no believer in dreams," continued Barbara, "but I cannot deny that these, which take such a hold upon mamma, do bear upon the case in a curious manner--the one she had last night especially." "What was it?" asked Mr. Carlyle. Well this kind you height have gravy XXX. " They both have wooly coats just like their baby lamb. " Title: How to Direct Your Units to a Specific Location in Age of Empires Age of Empires is a popular real-time strategy game that involves building your empire, gathering resources, researching technologies, and directing units to various locations. This tutorial will focus on the essential skill of moving your units to a specific location within the game world. By mastering this technique, you can efficiently manage your forces, strategically position them for battle, or guide them towards resource nodes. **Step 1:** Select Your Unit(s) To begin, click on the unit(s) you want to move by left-clicking once on the individual unit or using a box selection (dragging the mouse over multiple units). A red border should appear around the selected unit(s), indicating they are ready to receive orders. Key tip: Remember that some units have different movement speeds; ensure you're aware of their capabilities before issuing commands. Guidelines: Keep an eye on the mini-map to quickly locate your units if needed. If you select a single unit, its current hit points, attack strength, and range indicators will be displayed near the bottom center of the screen. **Step 2:** Choose the Destination Point Next, determine where you would like your selected unit(s) to go. You can either left-click directly on the terrain at the desired destination point or utilize the map overview by right-clicking on the minimap. The latter option allows you to set destinations more accurately without panning across large distances. Key tip: When selecting a destination point, consider potential obstacles such as trees, cliffs, or enemy troops. Make sure there is a clear path for your units to reach their target location. Guidelines: To cancel the order, press the 'Esc' key or right-click anywhere else on the map. **Step 3:** Confirm the Order Upon choosing a valid destination point, a light blue pathfinding line will appear, illustrating the route your unit(s) will take. At this stage, simply release the mouse button to confirm the order. Your unit(s) should start moving toward the designated area immediately. Key tip: If you wish to split up groups of units, hold down the Shift key while giving separate movement orders to each subgroup. Guidelines: Be mindful of formations when directing multiple units. Pressing 'H' will arrange your units in a defensive formation, while 'F' will spread them out for maximum visibility. **Step 4:** Monitor Progress & Adjust if Necessary Lastly, keep track of your unit(s)' progress towards the specified location. They may encounter unexpected challenges along the way, necessitating adjustments to their course or additional support from other units. Key tip: Use the idle villager hotkey ('I') to quickly identify any unassigned workers and assign them tasks. Guidelines: Regularly check on the status of your units, especially during combat situations. Issuing rally points for newly trained military units can help streamline their deployment and minimize micromanagement. By following these steps, you'll become proficient in guiding your units to specific locations in Age of Empires. With practice, this fundamental ability will enable better control over your empire, leading to improved strategic decision-making and ultimately, victory! She peered into the chest, and was surprised to recognize something glimmering in the shadows. She opened the chest, and found a beautiful map! The girl's eyes lit up with wonder. With this magical map, she could navigate to any place she wanted. She quickly tucked the map into her pocket and hurried off on many exciting adventures. Start a sentence with "So..." when you want to re-state or summarize what someone said: You can also use it when you want to point out the effects that something will have, like in this example: A: I'm so sleepy. I'm going to bed now. B: So you're not going to help me clean up? "All for nothing" describes work that you did, which didn't have an effect or won't be used. In the example above, the speaker did a lot of work that won't be used in the final version of the project. Here's another example: They banged and banged on the door for hours, but it was all for nothing. There was no one there to hear them. Anna and Ben like to help their mom cook. They have their own equipment: a small pot, a spoon, a bowl and a apron. Mom lets them cook some soup with carrots, potatoes and water. "Can I put some salt in the soup?" Ben asks mom. "Yes, but only a little bit. Salt makes food tasty, but too much salt makes food bitter," mom says. Anna wants to put some salt too. She takes the salt shaker and shakes it over the pot. But she shakes it too hard and a lot of salt falls into the soup. "Oh no, Anna, you put too much salt!" Ben says. Anna feels sorry. She did not mean to put too much salt. She hopes the soup is still good. Mom tastes the soup. She makes a face. The soup is very bitter. "I'm sorry, mom. I put too much salt," Anna says. "It's okay, Anna. It was an accident. We can fix the soup. We can add some more water and some milk. That will make the soup less bitter," mom says. She adds some water and some milk to the pot and stirs the soup. She tastes it again. The soup is better now. It is not bitter anymore. "Good job, mom. The soup is yummy now," Ben says. Anna smiles.
