Program Arcade GamesWith Python And Pygame Now that you can create loops, it is time to move on to learning how to create graphics. This chapter covers: - How the computer handles x, y coordinates. It isn't like the coordinate system you learned in math class. - How to specify colors. With millions of colors to choose from, telling the computer what color to use isn't as easy as just saying "red." - How to open a blank window for drawing. Every artist needs a canvas. - How to draw lines, rectangles, ellipses, and arcs. The Cartesian coordinate system, shown in Figure 5.1 (Wikimedia Commons), is the system most people are used to when plotting graphics. This is the system taught in school. The computer uses a similar, but somewhat different, coordinate system. Understanding why it is different requires a quick bit of computer history. During the early '80s, most computer systems were text-based and did not support graphics. Figure 5.2 (Wikimedia Commons) shows an early spreadsheet program run on an Apple ][ computer that was popular in the '80s. When positioning text on the screen, programmers started at the top calling it line 1. The screen continued down for 24 lines and across for 40 characters. Even with plain text, it was possible to make rudimentary graphics by just using characters on the keyboard. See this kitten shown in Figure 5.3 and look carefully at how it is drawn. When making this art, characters were still positioned starting with line 1 at the top. Later the character set was expanded to include boxes and other primitive drawing shapes. Characters could be drawn in different colors. As shown in Figure 5.4 the graphics got more advanced. Search the web for "ASCII art" and many more examples can be found. Once computers moved to being able to control individual pixels for graphics, the text-based coordinate system stuck. The $x$ coordinates work the same as the Cartesian coordinates system. But the $y$ coordinates are reversed. Rather than the zero $y$ coordinate at the bottom of the graph like in Cartesian graphics, the zero $y$ coordinate is at the top of the screen with the computer. As the $y$ values go up, the computer coordinate position moved down the screen, just like lines of text rather than standard Cartesian graphics. See Figure 5.5. Also, note the screen covers the lower right quadrant, where the Cartesian coordinate system usually focuses on the upper right quadrant. It is possible to draw items at negative coordinates, but they will be drawn off-screen. This can be useful when part of a shape is off screen. The computer figures out what is off-screen and the programmer does not need to worry too much about it. To make graphics easier to work with, we'll use the Pygame. Pygame is a library of code other people have written, and makes it simple to: - Draw graphic shapes - Display bitmapped images - Interact with keyboard, mouse, and gamepad - Play sound - Detect when objects collide The first code a Pygame program needs to do is load and initialize the Pygame library. Every program that uses Pygame should start with these lines: # Import a library of functions called 'pygame' import pygame # Initialize the game engine pygame.init() If you haven't installed Pygame yet, directions for installing Pygame are available in the before you begin section. If Pygame is not installed on your computer, you will get an error when trying to run import pygame. Important: The import pygame looks for a library file named pygame. If a programmer creates a new program named pygame.py, the computer will import that file instead! This will prevent any pygame programs from working until that pygame.py file is deleted. Next, we need to add variables that define our program's colors. Colors are defined in a list of three colors: red, green, and blue. Have you ever heard of an RGB monitor? This is where the term comes. Red-Green-Blue. With older monitors, you could sit really close to the monitor and make out the individual RGB colors. At least before your mom told you not to sit so close to the TV. This is hard to do with today's high resolution monitors. Each element of the RGB triad is a number ranging from 0 to 255. Zero means there is none of the color, and 255 tells the monitor to display as much of the color as possible. The colors combine in an additive way, so if all three colors are specified, the color on the monitor appears white. (This is different than how ink and paint work.) Lists in Python are surrounded by either square brackets or parentheses. (Chapter 7 covers lists in detail and the difference between the two types.) Individual numbers in the list are separated by commas. Below is an example that creates variables and sets them equal to lists of three numbers. These lists will be used later to specify colors. # Define some colors black = ( 0, 0, 0) white = ( 255, 255, 255) green = ( 0, 255, 0) red = ( 255, 0, 0) Using the interactive shell in IDLE, try defining these variables and printing them If the five colors above aren't the colors you are looking for, you can define your own. To pick a color, find an on-line "color picker" like the one shown in Figure 5.6. One such color picker is at: Extra: Some color pickers specify colors in hexadecimal. You can enter hexadecimal numbers if you start them with 0x. For example: white = (0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF) Eventually the program will need to use the value of $\pi$ when drawing arcs, so this is a good time in our program to define a variable that contains the value of $\pi$. (It is also possible to import this from the math library as math.pi.) pi = 3.141592653 So far, the programs we have created only printed text out to the screen. Those programs did not open any windows like most modern programs do. The code to open a window is not complex. Below is the required code, which creates a window sized to a width of 700 pixels, and a height of 500: # Set the width and height of the screen size = (700,500) screen = pygame.display.set_mode(size) Why set_mode? Why not open_window? The reason is that this command can actually do a lot more than open a window. It can also create games that run in a full-screen mode. This removes the start menu, title bars, and gives the game control of everything on the screen. Because this mode is slightly more complex to use, and most people prefer windowed games anyway, we'll skip a detailed discussion on full-screen games. But if you want to find out more about full-screen games, check out the documentation on pygame's display command. Also, why size=(700,500) and not size=700,500? The same reason why we put parentheses around the color definitions. Python can't normally store two numbers (a height and width) into one variable. The only way it can is if the numbers are stored as a list. Lists need either parentheses or square brackets. (Technically, parenthesis surrounding a set of numbers is more accurately called a tuple or an immutable list. Lists surrounded by square brackets are just called lists. An experienced Python developer would cringe at calling a list of numbers surrounded by parentheses a list rather than a tuple.) Lists are covered in detail in Chapter 7. To set the title of the window (which shown in the title bar) use the following line of code: pygame.display.set_caption("Professor Craven's Cool Game") With just the code written so far, the program would create a window and immediately hang. The user can't interact with the window, even to close it. All of this needs to be programmed. Code needs to be added so that the program waits in a loop until the user clicks "exit." This is the most complex part of the program, and a complete understanding of it isn't needed yet. But it is necessary to have an idea of what it does, so spend some time studying it and asking questions. #Loop until the user clicks the close button. done = False # Used to manage how fast the screen updates clock = pygame.time.Clock() # -------- Main Program Loop ----------- while done == False: # ALL EVENT PROCESSING SHOULD GO BELOW THIS COMMENT for event in pygame.event.get(): # User did something if event.type == pygame.QUIT: # If user clicked close done = True # Flag that we are done so we exit this loop # ALL EVENT PROCESSING SHOULD GO ABOVE THIS COMMENT # ALL GAME LOGIC SHOULD GO BELOW THIS COMMENT # ALL GAME LOGIC SHOULD GO ABOVE THIS COMMENT # ALL CODE TO DRAW SHOULD GO BELOW THIS COMMENT # ALL CODE TO DRAW SHOULD GO ABOVE THIS COMMENT # Limit to 20 frames per second clock.tick(20) Eventually we will add code to handle the keyboard and mouse clicks. That code will go between the comments for event processing. Code for determining when bullets are fired and how objects move will go between the comments for game logic. We'll talk about that in later chapters. Code to draw will go in between the appropriate draw-code comments. Alert! One of the most frustrating problems programmers have is to mess up the event processing loop. This "event processing" code handles all the keystrokes, mouse button clicks, and several other types of events. For example your loop might look like: for event in pygame.event.get(): if event.type == pygame.QUIT: print("User asked to quit.") if event.type == pygame.KEYDOWN: print("User pressed a key.") if event.type == pygame.KEYUP: print("User let go of a key.") if event.type == pygame.MOUSEBUTTONDOWN: print("User pressed a mouse button") The events (like pressing keys) all go together in a list. The program uses a for loop to loop through each event. Using a chain of if statements the code figures out what type of event occured, and the code to handle that event goes in the if statement. All the if statements should go together, in one for loop. A common mistake when doing copy and pasting of code is to not merge loops from two programs, but to have two event loops. # Here is one event loop for event in pygame.event.get(): if event.type == pygame.QUIT: print("User asked to quit.") if event.type == pygame.KEYDOWN: print("User pressed a key.") if event.type == pygame.KEYUP: print("User let go of a key.") # Here the programmer has copied another event loop # into the program. This is BAD. The events were already # processed. for event in pygame.event.get(): if event.type == pygame.QUIT: print("User asked to quit.") if event.type == pygame.MOUSEBUTTONDOWN: print("User pressed a mouse button") The for loop on line 2 grabbed all of the user events. The for loop on line 13 won't grab any events because they were already processed in the prior loop. Another typical problem is to start drawing, and then try to finish the event loop: for event in pygame.event.get(): if event.type == pygame.QUIT: print("User asked to quit.") if event.type == pygame.KEYDOWN: print("User pressed a key.") pygame.rect.draw(screen,green,[50,50,100,100]) # This is code that processes events. But it is not in the # 'for' loop that processes events. It will not act reliably. if event.type == pygame.KEYUP: print("User let go of a key.") if event.type == pygame.MOUSEBUTTONDOWN: print("User pressed a mouse button") This will cause the program to ignore some keyboard and mouse commands. Why? The for loop processes all the events in a list. So if there are two keys that are hit, the for loop will process both. In the example above, the if statements are not in the for loop. If there are multiple events, the if statements will only run for the last event, rather than all events. The basic logic and order for each frame of the game: - While not done: - For each event (keypress, mouse click, etc.): - Use a chain of if statements to run code to handle each event. - Run calculations to determine where objects move, what happens when objects collide, etc. - Clear the screen - Draw everything - For each event (keypress, mouse click, etc.): It makes the program easier to read and understand if these steps aren't mixed togther. Don't do some calculations, some drawing, some more calculations, some more drawing. Also, see how this is similar to the calculator done in chapter one. Get user input, run calculations, and output the answer. That same pattern applies here. The code for drawing the image to the screen happens inside the while loop. With the clock tick set at 10, the contents of the window will be drawn 10 times per second. If it happens too fast the computer is sluggish because all of its time is spent updating the screen. If it isn't in the loop at all, the screen won't redraw properly. If the drawing is outside the loop, the screen may initially show the graphics, but the graphics won't reappear if the window is minimized, or if another window is placed in front. Right now, clicking the "close" button of a window while running this Pygame program in IDLE will still cause the program to crash. This is a hassle because it requires a lot of clicking to close a crashed program. The problem is, even though the loop has exited, the program hasn't told the computer to close the window. By calling the command below, the program will close any open windows and exit as desired. The following code clears whatever might be in the window with a white background. Remember that the variable white was defined earlier as a list of 3 RGB values. # Clear the screen and set the screen background screen.fill(white) This should be done before any drawing command is issued. Clearing the screen after the program draws graphics results in the user only seeing a blank screen. When a window is first created it has a black background. It is still important to clear the screen because there are several things that could occur to keep this window from starting out cleared. A program should not assume it has a blank canvas to draw on. Very important! You must flip the display after you draw. The computer will not display the graphics as you draw them because it would cause the screen to flicker. This waits to display the screen until the program has finished drawing. The command below "flips" the graphics to the screen. Failure to include this command will mean the program just shows a blank screen. Any drawing code after this flip will not display. # Go ahead and update the screen with what we've drawn. pygame.display.flip() Let's bring everything we've talked about into one full program. This code can be used as a base template for a Pygame program. It opens up a blank window and waits for the user to press the close button. # Sample Python/Pygame Programs # Simpson College Computer Science # http://programarcadegames.com/ # http://simpson.edu/computer-science/ # Explanation video: http://youtu.be/vRB_983kUMc import pygame # Define some colors black = ( 0, 0, 0) white = ( 255, 255, 255) green = ( 0, 255, 0) red = ( 255, 0, 0) pygame.init() # Set the width and height of the screen [width,height] size = [700,500] screen = pygame.display.set_mode(size) pygame.display.set_caption("My Game") #Loop until the user clicks the close button. done = False # Used to manage how fast the screen updates clock = pygame.time.Clock() # -------- Main Program Loop ----------- while done == False: # ALL EVENT PROCESSING SHOULD GO BELOW THIS COMMENT for event in pygame.event.get(): # User did something if event.type == pygame.QUIT: # If user clicked close done = True # Flag that we are done so we exit this loop # ALL EVENT PROCESSING SHOULD GO ABOVE THIS COMMENT # ALL GAME LOGIC SHOULD GO BELOW THIS COMMENT # ALL GAME LOGIC SHOULD GO ABOVE THIS COMMENT # ALL CODE TO DRAW SHOULD GO BELOW THIS COMMENT # First, clear the screen to white. Don't put other drawing commands # above this, or they will be erased with this command. screen.fill(white) # ALL CODE TO DRAW SHOULD GO ABOVE THIS COMMENT # Go ahead and update the screen with what we've drawn. pygame.display.flip() # Limit to 20 frames per second clock.tick(20) # Close the window and quit. # If you forget this line, the program will 'hang' # on exit if running from IDLE. pygame.quit() Here is a list of things that you can draw: A program can draw things like rectangles, polygons, circles, ellipses, arcs, and lines. We will also cover how to display text with graphics. Bitmapped graphics such as images are covered in Chapter 12. If you decide to look at that pygame reference, you might see a function definition like this: pygame.draw.rect(Surface, color, Rect, width=0): return Rect A frequent cause of confusion is the part of the line that says width=0. What this means is that if you do not supply a width, it will default to zero. Thus this function call: pygame.draw.rect(screen, red, [55,500,10,5]) Is the same as this function call: pygame.draw.rect(screen, red, [55,500,10,5], 0) The : return Rect is telling you that the function returns a rectangle, the same one that was passed in. You can just ignore this part. What will not work, is attempting to copy the line and put width=0 in the quotes. # This fails and the error the computer gives you is # really hard to understand. pygame.draw.rect(screen, red, [55,500,10,5], width=0) The code example below shows how to draw a line on the screen. It will draw on the screen a green line from (0,0) to (100,100) that is 5 pixels wide. Remember that green is a variable that was defined earlier as a list of three RGB values. # Draw on the screen a green line from (0,0) to (100,100) # that is 5 pixels wide. pygame.draw.line(screen,green,[0,0],[100,100],5) Use the base template from the prior example and add the code to draw lines. Read the comments to figure out exactly where to put the code. Try drawing lines with different thicknesses, colors, and locations. Draw several lines. Programs can repeat things over and over. The next code example draws a line over and over using a loop. Programs can use this technique to do multiple lines, and even draw an entire car. Putting a line drawing command inside a loop will cause multiple lines being drawn to the screen. But here's the catch. If each line has the same starting and ending coordinates, then each line will draw on top of the other line. It will look like only one line was drawn. To get around this, it is necessary to offset the coordinates each time through the loop. So the first time through the loop the variable y_offset is zero. The line in the code below is drawn from (0,10) to (100,110). The next time through the loop y_offset increased by 10. This causes the next line to be drawn to have new coordinates of (0,20) and (100,120). This continues each time through the loop shifting the coordinates of each line down by 10 pixels. # Draw on the screen several green lines from (0,10) to (100,110) # 5 pixels wide using a while loop y_offset = 0 while y_offset < 100: pygame.draw.line(screen,red,[0,10+y_offset],[100,110+y_offset],5) y_offset = y_offset+10 This same code could be done even more easily with a for loop: # Draw on the screen several green lines from (0,10) to (100,110) # 5 pixels wide using a for loop for y_offset in range(0,100,10): pygame.draw.line(screen,red,[0,10+y_offset],[100,110+y_offset],5) Run this code and try using different changes to the offset. Try creating an offset with different values. Experiment with different values until exactly how this works is obvious. For example, here is a loop that uses sine and cosine to create a more complex set of offsets and produces the image shown in Figure 5.7. for i in range(200): radians_x = i/20 radians_y = i/6 x=int( 75 * math.sin(radians_x)) + 200 y=int( 75 * math.cos(radians_y)) + 200 pygame.draw.line(screen,black,[x,y],[x+5,y], 5) Multiple elements can be drawn in one for loop, such as this code which draws the multiple X's shown in Figure 5.8. for x_offset in range(30,300,30): pygame.draw.line(screen,black,[x_offset,100],[x_offset-10,90], 2 ) pygame.draw.line(screen,black,[x_offset,90],[x_offset-10,100], 2 ) When drawing a rectangle, the computer needs coordinates for the upper left rectangle corner (the origin), and a height and width. Figure 5.9 shows a rectangle (and an ellipse, which will be explained later) with the origin at (20,20), a width of 250 and a height of 100. When specifying a rectangle the computer needs a list of these four numbers in the order of (x, y, width, height). The next code example draws this rectangle. The first two numbers in the list define the upper left corner at (20,20). The next two numbers specify first the width of 250 pixels, and then the height of 100 pixels. The 2 at the end specifies a line width of 2 pixels. The larger the number, the thicker the line around the rectangle. If this number is 0, then there will not be a border around the rectangle. Instead it will be filled in with the color specified. # Draw a rectangle pygame.draw.rect(screen,black,[20,20,250,100],2) An ellipse is drawn just like a rectangle. The boundaries of a rectangle are specified, and the computer draws an ellipses inside those boundaries. The most common mistake in working with an ellipse is to think that the starting point specifies the center of the ellipse. In reality, nothing is drawn at the starting point. It is the upper left of a rectangle that contains the ellipse. Looking back at Figure 5.9 one can see an ellipse 250 pixels wide and 100 pixels tall. The upper left corner of the 250x100 rectangle that contains it is at (20,20). Note that nothing is actually drawn at (20,20). With both drawn on top of each other it is easier to see how the ellipse is specified. # Draw an ellipse, using a rectangle as the outside boundaries pygame.draw.ellipse(screen,black,[20,20,250,100],2) What if a program only needs to draw part of an ellipse? That can be done with the arc command. This command is similar to the ellipse command, but it includes start and end angles for the arc to be drawn. The angles are in radians. The code example below draws four arcs showing four difference quadrants of the circle. Each quadrant is drawn in a different color to make the arcs sections easier to see. The result of this code is shown in Figure 5.10. # Draw an arc as part of an ellipse. Use radians to determine what # angle to draw. pygame.draw.arc(screen,green,[100,100,250,200], pi/2, pi, 2) pygame.draw.arc(screen,black,[100,100,250,200], 0, pi/2, 2) pygame.draw.arc(screen,red, [100,100,250,200],3*pi/2, 2*pi, 2) pygame.draw.arc(screen,blue, [100,100,250,200], pi, 3*pi/2, 2) The next line of code draws a polygon. The triangle shape is defined with three points at (100,100) (0,200) and (200,200). It is possible to list as many points as desired. Note how the points are listed. Each point is a list of two numbers, and the points themselves are nested in another list that holds all the points. This code draws what can be seen in Figure 5.11. # This draws a triangle using the polygon command pygame.draw.polygon(screen,black,[[100,100],[0,200],[200,200]],5) Text is slightly more complex. There are three things that need to be done. First, the program creates a variable that holds information about the font to be used, such as what typeface and how big. Second, the program creates an image of the text. One way to think of it is that the program carves out a "stamp" with the required letters that is ready to be dipped in ink and stamped on the paper. The third thing that is done is the program tells where this image of the text should be stamped (or "blit'ed") to the screen. Here's an example: # Select the font to use. Default font, 25 pt size. font = pygame.font.Font(None, 25) # Render the text. "True" means anti-aliased text. # Black is the color. The variable black was defined # above as a list of [0,0,0] # Note: This line creates an image of the letters, # but does not put it on the screen yet. text = font.render("My text",True,black) # Put the image of the text on the screen at 250x250 screen.blit(text, [250,250]) Want to print the score to the screen? That is a bit more complex. This does not work: text = font.render("Score: ",score,True,black) Why? A program can't just add extra items to font.render like the print statement. Only one string can be sent to the command, therefore the actual value of score needs to be appended to the "Score: " string. But this doesn't work either: text = font.render("Score: "+score,True,black) If score is an integer variable, the computer doesn't know how to add it to a string. You, the programmer, must convert the score to a string. Then add the strings together like this: text = font.render("Score: "+str(score),True,black) Now you know how to print the score. If you want to print a timer, that requires print formatting, discussed in a chapter later on. Check in the example code for section on-line for the timer.py example: This is a full listing of the program discussed in this chapter. This program, along with other programs, may be downloaded from: # Sample Python/Pygame Programs # Simpson College Computer Science # http://programarcadegames.com/ # http://simpson.edu/computer-science/ # Import a library of functions called 'pygame' import pygame # Initialize the game engine pygame.init() # Define the colors we will use in RGB format black = [ 0, 0, 0] white = [255,255,255] blue = [ 0, 0,255] green = [ 0,255, 0] red = [255, 0, 0] pi = 3.141592653 # Set the height and width of the screen size = [400,500] screen = pygame.display.set_mode(size) pygame.display.set_caption("Professor Craven's Cool Game") #Loop until the user clicks the close button. done = False clock = pygame.time.Clock() while done == False: # This limits the while loop to a max of 10 times per second. # Leave this out and we will use all CPU we can. clock.tick(10) for event in pygame.event.get(): # User did something if event.type == pygame.QUIT: # If user clicked close done=True # Flag that we are done so we exit this loop # All drawing code happens after the for loop and but # inside the main while done==False loop. # Clear the screen and set the screen background screen.fill(white) # Draw on the screen a green line from (0,0) to (100,100) # 5 pixels wide. pygame.draw.line(screen,green,[0,0],[100,100],5) # Draw on the screen several green lines from (0,10) to (100,110) # 5 pixels wide using a loop for y_offset in range(0,100,10): pygame.draw.line(screen,red,[0,10+y_offset],[100,110+y_offset],5) # Draw a rectangle pygame.draw.rect(screen,black,[20,20,250,100],2) # Draw an ellipse, using a rectangle as the outside boundaries pygame.draw.ellipse(screen,black,[20,20,250,100],2) # Draw an arc as part of an ellipse. # Use radians to determine what angle to draw. pygame.draw.arc(screen,black,[20,220,250,200], 0, pi/2, 2) pygame.draw.arc(screen,green,[20,220,250,200], pi/2, pi, 2) pygame.draw.arc(screen,blue, [20,220,250,200], pi,3*pi/2, 2) pygame.draw.arc(screen,red, [20,220,250,200],3*pi/2, 2*pi, 2) # This draws a triangle using the polygon command pygame.draw.polygon(screen,black,[[100,100],[0,200],[200,200]],5) # Select the font to use. Default font, 25 pt size. font = pygame.font.Font(None, 25) # Render the text. "True" means anti-aliased text. # Black is the color. This creates an image of the # letters, but does not put it on the screen text = font.render("My text",True,black) # Put the image of the text on the screen at 250x250 screen.blit(text, [250,250]) # Go ahead and update the screen with what we've drawn. # This MUST happen after all the other drawing commands. pygame.display.flip() # Be IDLE friendly pygame.quit() Click here for a multiple-choice review quiz. I was going to put this on top. Don't take it [objects on black base] all down. An astrolabe is an ancient Greek tool for measuring star positions. It was so effective that it was used by mariners even past the time of Columbus and into the 1700s. First off you need to create an astrolabe. Fortunately, the University of California Berkley has a cut out design with steps on how to put together the finishing touches (or you can use a protractor). Then one needs to know how to read the astrolabe. The next page of the guide offers insight. Long story short: use straw/tube to look at object, where the string crosses the scale is the height of angle in degrees. Now that you have a working astrolabe let us do some geography and astronomy. Finding Latitude (For Northern Hemisphere) First off find the big dipper in the sky at night. Make a line connect the far two stars of the cup. Continue that line until you see a medium bright star. That is Polaris, the north star. Polaris is currently hovering around the north pole (not exactly but close enough) to be used as a reference for ancient scientists, mariners, and you. Here's a helpful image to show what you are looking for. Now that you know where Polaris is use the astrolabe to determine the degrees in height it is. The answer you get is your latitude . This is the number of degrees you are from the equator. Take 90 and subtract your number and this is the number of degrees you are from the North Pole. Charting the Stars Pick out a star, preferably one in a constellation you can quickly find. Determine the height in degrees with the astrolabe. Do more measurements 30 minutes, 60 minutes, and 90 minutes later. See how the movement is like clockwork? That it is because the stars are not moving but the Earth is at a steady pace. Try estimating where the stars will be in 30 minutes. Remember, the horizon is 180 degrees and the Earth rotates 360 degrees. Try estimating where the stars will be tomorrow at a certain time and check your results. The foreskin is a fold of skin that covers the end of the penis. Cutting off the foreskin is called circumcision. The foreskin is connected to the head of the penis. During circumcision, the foreskin is removed from the penis. Circumcision is usually performed on infant males for medical, religious or cultural reasons. Circumcision is common in some parts of the world, such as the United States and the Middle East, but not common in some other parts of the world, such as Europe and China. Title: How to Get a Horse Fit Introduction: This tutorial will provide you with comprehensive information about getting your horse fit. A fit horse is essential for optimal performance in various disciplines such as jumping, dressage, endurance riding, or trail riding. The process involves proper nutrition, exercise regimen, rest, and regular veterinary care. This guide aims to help both beginner and experienced equestrians understand the steps required to get their horses in top physical condition. Step 1: Consult Your Veterinarian Before starting any fitness program for your horse, consult with your vet. They can evaluate your horse's overall health status, identify potential issues that may hinder training progress, recommend appropriate exercises based on breed, age, and current level of fitness, and suggest dietary changes if necessary. Key Tip: Be honest about your horse's history, including previous injuries or illnesses, so your vet can give accurate advice tailored specifically for your animal companion. Step 2: Design an Exercise Program Depending on your horse's discipline, design an exercise routine focusing on building strength, stamina, flexibility, and coordination. For example, a show jumper might require more explosive power while a dressage horse would benefit from greater control and precision. Remember, gradual progression is crucial to prevent injury. Guideline: Start with light workouts (like walking) then gradually increase intensity and duration over time. Always warm up before intense sessions and cool down afterwards. Step 3: Feed Appropriately Proper nutrition plays a vital role in maintaining a healthy weight and energy levels during exercise. Balanced meals should include good quality roughages like hay or pasture along with grains or concentrates depending upon the horse's needs. It's advisable to seek guidance from a certified equine nutritionist or your vet when making significant changes to your horse's diet. Key Tip: Avoid sudden shifts in feed type or quantity; instead, introduce new elements slowly over several days or weeks. Step 4: Provide Regular Rest Rest periods are just as important as workout sessions because they allow muscles to recover and rebuild. Overworking a horse without sufficient downtime increases the risk of fatigue-related accidents and injuries. Guideline: Schedule at least one full day off per week and vary the types of activities throughout the week to ensure balanced muscle development. Step 5: Monitor Progress Keep track of your horse's progress through regular weigh-ins, body score assessments, gait analysis, and behavioral observations. These records serve as valuable tools to adjust training programs according to individual needs. Key Tip: Body scoring charts can be used to estimate whether your horse is carrying too much or too little weight, helping maintain ideal conditions for peak performance. Step 6: Maintain Dental Health Healthy teeth directly impact digestion and therefore nutrient absorption. Routine dental examinations every six months ensure there are no sharp points or hooks causing discomfort or interfering with chewing efficiency. Step 7: Stay Updated on Vaccinations and Deworming Schedules Regular vaccinations protect against diseases, while deworming prevents internal parasites from compromising your horse's immune system and overall wellbeing. Follow recommended schedules provided by your veterinarian. In conclusion, achieving optimum fitness for your horse requires careful planning, execution, monitoring, and adaptation. By following these steps meticulously, you'll have a strong, agile, and happy partner ready to take on any challenge! [Disney Phone says "my name is Minnie Mouse"] I want' 'a come on these lines. Did I see who wiped it on there? You said you go on the train back? [Med shows her the finished chain of pop beads] What other kind of animals do you think live with him? It's halfway between my house and your house. 'Water treatment' involves filtering the water through sand beds and then adding a chemical to kill any remaining organisms. It was pouring hard coming down hard. Title: How to Clip Quail Wings Clipping quail wings is an important procedure if you plan to keep them as pets or raise them for meat production. This process prevents quails from flying away and getting injured. It's crucial to handle this task with care and understanding to avoid causing unnecessary stress or harm to your birds. Here's our comprehensive guide on clipping quail wings. **Things You Need:** 1. A pair of sharp scissors (preferably blunt-nosed) 2. A calm, quiet workspace 3. One or more quails 4. Treats to reward your quails **Step 1: Familiarize Yourself With Quail Anatomy** Before starting, familiarize yourself with a quail's anatomy. Each wing has ten primary feathers - these are the longest ones at the end of the wing. Our goal is to clip these without cutting into the blood feathers. Blood feathers have a vein running through them which can cause bleeding if cut. **Step 2: Catch Your Quail Gently** Approach your quail slowly and gently pick them up using both hands. Place one hand around its body, just above the legs, while supporting its breast with the other hand. Be patient; it might take some practice before you get comfortable handling your quails. **Step 3: Identify The Primary Feathers** Turn your quail onto its back so that you can see its underside. Now look at the folded wings. Locate the large flight feathers near the tip of each wing. These are the primaries we need to clip. **Step 4: Start Clipping** Using your sharp scissors, start trimming the visible part of the primary feathers. Try not to go beyond where they attach to the secondary feathers. Remember, do not rush! Take your time to ensure clean cuts. **Key Tips For Steps 3 & 4:** * Only clip about half to two-thirds of the length of the primary feathers. If you remove too much, it could hinder their balance and ability to maneuver on ground level. * Avoid cutting into the smaller, greyish colored feathers underneath. Those are the blood feathers mentioned earlier. * Always use clean, sharp scissors to make neat cuts and reduce risk of infection. **Step 5: Check For Bleeding** After clipping, check immediately for any signs of bleeding. Though rare, sometimes a blood feather may be accidentally nicked during the process. If there is bleeding, dab the area lightly with cornstarch until it stops. **Step 6: Return Quails To Their Habitat** Once everything looks good, return your quails to their habitat. Offer them treats as a positive reinforcement and distraction after this potentially stressful experience. **Additional Guidelines:** * Never clip only one wing. This will unbalance the bird and make it harder for them to land properly, leading to potential injuries. * Wait until your quails are fully grown before attempting to clip their wings. Young quails still developing their flight skills should not undergo this procedure. * Regularly inspect clipped wings for new growth. Depending on the species, reclipping might be necessary every few months. Remember, practice makes perfect. Don't worry if you don't succeed perfectly the first time. As long as you proceed carefully and respectfully, your quails will soon adjust to their newly clipped condition. Do you know how to change a tire? Track Taste Buds Stick your tongue out. Look at it in a mirror. Do you see small bumps all over your tongue? Those small bumps are taste buds. They are the parts of your tongue that help you taste the food you eat. Do the activity below to find out how your taste buds react. - lemon juice - 8 cotton swabs - 2 glasses of water Test your taste buds. Ask an adult family member to test his or her taste buds, too. Dip a moistened cotton swab in sugar. Touch the swab to the front, back, and sides of your tongue. Note the part of the tongue where you sense a sweet taste most clearly. Then rinse your mouth with water. Repeat the procedure for salt, lemon juice, and coffee. Where in your mouth did you most clearly sense each of these tastes: sweet, salty, sour, and bitter? Label the drawing of the tongue above to show where taste buds for each taste are located. He built a small house called a cocoon around himself. Title: How to Build a Chopper Motorcycle Introduction: Building a chopper motorcycle is an exciting and rewarding project that allows you to create a customized bike tailored to your preferences. This comprehensive guide will walk you through the process, providing detailed steps and helpful tips along the way. Before starting this ambitious task, ensure you have sufficient knowledge about motorcycles, their components, and basic mechanical skills. Safety should always be your top priority; wear protective gear when working with tools and heavy machinery. I. Gather Necessary Tools and Materials * Basic hand tools (wrenches, sockets, pliers) * Power tools (drill, grinder, saws) * Welding equipment * Metalworking tools (tubing bender, shears) * Frame jig or sturdy workbench * Engine assembly stand * Motorcycle engine (preferably V-twin) * Motorcycle transmission * Custom frame kit or raw materials (steel tubing) * Front end assembly (springer fork or telescopic) * Wheels (spoked or magnesium) * Tires (dual sports or cruiser style) * Brakes (hydraulic disc brakes recommended) * Gas tank * Seat * Exhaust system * Electrical wiring harness * Battery * Headlight, taillight, turn signals * Mirrors * Controls (handlebars, footpegs, shifters) * Miscellaneous hardware (nuts, bolts, washers) II. Design Your Chopper 1. Choose a theme or inspiration for your build. Consider factors such as color scheme, styling cues, and overall aesthetic. Sketch out rough ideas on paper before committing to any major design decisions. 2. Determine the wheelbase length by measuring from the center of the front hub to the center of the rear axle. Adjust accordingly based on your desired ride height and handling characteristics. 3. Decide upon the rake angle, which affects steering stability and responsiveness. A larger rake angle results in slower steering but increased straight-line stability. 4. Calculate the trail value using the formula Trail = Rake * Wheel Radius / 1000. The optimal trail range varies between 90mm and 150mm depending on personal preference and intended usage. III. Assemble the Frame 1. Begin constructing the main downtube section first, followed by the neck and backbone sections. Use a frame jig or securely clamp pieces together while welding to maintain alignment. 2. Install the motor mounts at appropriate locations along the backbone according to manufacturer specifications. Make sure they are level and parallel for proper engine placement. 3. Attach the front downtube and reinforce it with gussets if necessary. Connect the neck tube and ensure all joints fit snugly against one another. 4. Weld reinforcing plates around critical junctions for added strength and rigidity. 5. Drill holes for brake lines, fuel lines, electrical wiring, and other miscellaneous fittings prior to finalizing the frame assembly. IV. Prepare the Engine and Transmission [takes another cup out of the bag] Yes it looks like your brother chewed it. Well we have a couple o' letters we could write. Title: How to Copy and Paste an Image in PaintShop Pro PaintShop Pro is a powerful image editing software that allows you to manipulate and edit digital images. One basic yet essential function in this program is copying and pasting an image or parts of an image. This tutorial will provide a comprehensive guide on how to perform these actions with clear explanations, helpful tips, and useful guidelines. **Step 1: Open Your Source Image** Start by launching PaintShop Pro on your computer. Once open, select "File" from the top menu bar then choose "Open." Navigate through your files until you find the image you want to copy, highlight it, and click "Open." The image should now appear on the main workspace. **Step 2: Select the Area You Want to Copy (Optional)** If you only wish to copy a specific portion of the image instead of the entire thing, you'll need to make a selection first. To do so, ensure the "Selection Tools" icon is active (it looks like a dotted square). Click and drag your cursor over the area you want to select. Release the mouse button when you have highlighted the desired section. Key Tips: Use the Shift key while selecting to add more areas; use Ctrl + Shift keys together to subtract from the current selection. **Step 3: Copy the Selected Area** After making your selection, go to the "Edit" tab located at the top of the interface. From the drop-down list, click on "Copy." Alternatively, press Ctrl+C on your keyboard as a shortcut. By doing this, you are storing the selected pixels into the clipboard memory of the application. **Step 4: Create a New Image File** Now that you have copied the necessary part, create a new file where you can paste the copied content. Go back to the "File" menu and click on "New." A dialog box will pop up asking about the size of the new canvas. Generally, leave all settings as default unless you have particular dimensions in mind. Confirm by clicking "OK". **Step 5: Pasting the Copied Content** With the new blank image ready, navigate again to the "Edit" tab. This time, click on "Paste," or simply hit Ctrl+V. As expected, the previously copied material will be inserted into the new image. If you had made a partial selection earlier, only those specific sections would show up here. **Step 6: Positioning and Adjustments** By default, the pasted content may not align perfectly within the new image boundaries. Move it around using the "Move Tool" (looks like a cross arrow) found under the "Tools" palette. Additionally, resize if needed via the "Image" > "Resize" option. Remember to maintain aspect ratio to prevent distortion. **Step 7: Save Your Work** Finally, save your newly created image. Head to "File" > "Save As..." Choose your preferred location, input a name, select a format such as JPEG or PNG, then confirm with "Save." Depending on the chosen format, further compression or quality adjustment options might become available before finalizing the saving process. In conclusion, mastering simple tasks like copying and pasting in PaintShop Pro opens doors to many creative possibilities. Understanding these fundamentals gives users confidence and serves as a foundation for exploring advanced features offered by the software. Do you want to play with this bread? One tool that you can use to create such galleries is A4Flash Photo Gallery Builder. This tutorial will guide you through creating a professional-looking photo gallery using this software. Step 1: Download and Install A4Flash Photo Gallery Builder The first step is to download A4Flash Photo Gallery Builder from their official website. Once you have downloaded the installation file, run it and follow the prompts to install the program onto your computer. After installation, launch the application. Key Tip: Make sure your system meets the minimum requirements specified by the developer before installing the software. Step 2: Select Your Preferred Template After opening the program, select 'New Project' on the main screen. You will then see several template options; choose one that suits your needs best. These templates are customizable, so don't worry if they aren't perfect right away. Guideline: Consider where you plan to post your gallery (website, social media, etc.) when choosing a template. Some platforms may require specific sizes or formats. Step 3: Add Photos to the Gallery Next, click on 'Add Images' to import the pictures you want to include in your gallery. Navigate to the location of these files on your device, select them all, and hit 'Open'. The selected images should now appear within the application. Key Tip: To ensure high image quality, always use original, high-resolution photos. However, remember to optimize them for web usage to avoid slow loading times. Step 4: Customize the Layout Now it's time to customize your gallery layout. Under the 'Layout' tab, you can adjust various settings like rows, columns, spacing, etc., according to your preference. Remember to maintain a balance between aesthetics and usability. Step 5: Create Thumbnail View Switch to the 'Thumbs' tab to configure your thumbnail view. Here, you can set the size, shape, border, and padding of your thumbnails. Also, decide whether you want to display text captions beneath each thumbnail. Step 6: Configure Slideshow Settings If you wish to add a slideshow feature, go to the 'Slide Show' tab. From here, you can control transition effects, speed, shuffle mode, fullscreen viewing, and more. Step 7: Apply Advanced Settings (Optional) For further customization, explore the 'Advanced' tab. Here, you can modify CSS styles, enable social sharing buttons, watermark your images, and much more. Step 8: Save and Publish Your Gallery Once you're satisfied with your creation, click 'Save & Publish'. Choose your preferred output format (HTML, Flash, ZIP), name your project, and specify a destination folder. Finally, hit 'Publish Now', and your new photo gallery will be generated! Key Tips: Always test your published gallery across different devices and browsers to ensure compatibility. Additionally, regularly back up your projects to prevent data loss. By following these steps, you'll have created a visually appealing and user-friendly photo gallery complete with thumbnails - no coding skills required! LESSON 4: Ready to solve the equation |Introduction to Elementary Algebra Help on solving algebra math problems Simple step by step method We are now ready to start solving the equation below. Solving means to know the value for 'x'. We know the value of 'x' when we can say: 'x' is equal to the left side of the equal sign. Most important rule: Because both sides of an equation are EQUAL, any operation we do on one side must be done on the other side. So let's start We already got an x of the left side. But we don't want the x on the right side. What can we do? We know from the previous lesson that we can make the +2x disappear if we use On the right side of the equation. Because it is the opposite operation. But remember: to keep things legal we must do the same thing on the left side also. Let's do it On the right side of the equal sign Is equal to zero. The equation becomes On the left side we can put the 'x's together like this It's easy to see that Now the equation becomes Which is the same as This is where we are now If we can make the -4 disappear we finally get the x alone on the left side of the equal sign and we can say that we solved the equation. The opposite of -4 is +4 so let's do it on BOTH sides. On the left side: -4 + 4 is equal to zero. On the right side: 7 + 4 is equal to 11. The equation is solved ! If we use 11 in place of 'x' we will get that both sides of the equation are EQUAL. In rapid motion... Bye for now. These blog posts are part of a program that is in development called Phonics the Easy Way. It is a phonics program that gives parents the information that they need in order to help their own kids with reading. These lessons are meant to be utilized with text that a parent is reading with their child vs. isolated skill and drills that do not transfer over into reading. These are the methods that I personally utilize in tutoring with my own students in reading online and I am able to increase their reading levels a full year with just 8-12 hours of instruction. So far we have taken a look at using the spot and dot method to figure out the patterns of words so that we can read more words that have two syllables. Today we are going to split up words that have three, four and five syllables to see how this process works. Here is a review of the Spot and Dot Rules. - Spot and dot each of the vowels. - Connect the dots and count the consonants in between the vowels. - If there is one consonant, then draw the line before. - If there are two consonants, then draw the line between them. - If there are three consonants, then draw the line between the consonant and the blend of digraph. Let's take a look at a 3 syllable word. First we spot all of the vowels for this word and they are a, e, i, and e. Next connect the dots and count the consonants in between the vowels. For the word advertise when you connect the a and the first e you will notice that there are two consonants, so draw a line between them both. Continue connecting the dots and counting the consonants. For the next set of dots there are also two consonants, so you will draw the line between the r and the t. Now it is time to identify where each of the parts goes on the chart. |Two vowels||C + le| As you can see ad- goes under closed, -ver- goes under bossy r, and -tise goes under silent e. After the child has identified where each of these parts goes on the chart, she is ready to put the word together and read advertise. Check out the video for a detailed example. Here is a list of other three syllable words that you can practice this strategy on. I go to office and then I came back. If you turn it this way it's a d. A decade (pronounce: DEK-aid) is a period of 10 years. The word comes from the Ancient Greek word for ten: "deka". 10 decades make a century, while 100 decades make a millennium. A decade is any 10 year period: There are different ways to refer to different groupings of ten years. For example, the period from 2001 to 2010 can be described as the 1st decade of the 21st century. An individual who has been alive two full decades is referred to as being in their 20s for the next decade of their life, from age 20 to 29. Decades are also considered specific groups of ten years sharing the same tens digit, identified by name, as in the nineteen-eighties (1980s) referring to the period from 1980 to 1989 and the nineteen-nineties (1990s) referring to the period from 1990 to 1999. This is the sense meant when someone refers to decades without other context, for example saying 'next decade' in any year between 2020 and 2029 would indicate the 2030s. Sometimes this is shortened to just the decade, where the context is clear, as in the eighties (80s) or the nineties (90s). No apostrophe is used before the s in the names of these decades. Tom and Lily were playing with their toy trucks in the garden. They had a big pile of sand and some boards to make ramps and bridges. They liked to load their trucks with sand and drive them over the boards. "Look, Lily, I can load more sand than you!" Tom said. He filled his truck with so much sand that it was very heavy. He tried to drive it over a board, but the board broke in half. The truck and the sand fell to the ground. "Oh no, Tom, you broke the board!" Lily said. She was sad because they had only two boards left. "Why did you load so much sand?" "Because I wanted to show you how strong I am," Tom said. He was sorry that he broke the board. He knew that Lily liked to play with the boards too. "But Tom, being strong is not only about loading a lot of sand," Lily said. "Being strong is also about being kind and sharing with others. If you load less sand, we can both play with the boards and have more fun." Tom thought about what Lily said. He realized that she was right. He said, "I'm sorry, Lily, you are very smart. I will load less sand and share the boards with you. Can we still be friends?" "Of course, Tom, we are always friends," Lily said. She smiled and gave him a hug. They picked up the broken board and the sand and put them away. Then they took their trucks and the other boards and played together happily. They learned that being strong is not only about how much you can load, but also about how much you can love. Who rides on top of the Baths? Sis get off the table and sit down. Drafting a Pet Protection Agreement: A Comprehensive Guide A pet protection agreement is a legal document that outlines the care and custody of your pets in the event of your incapacity or death. This guide will walk you through the process of drafting such an agreement, providing detailed explanations and helpful tips along the way. By following these steps, you can ensure that your beloved pets are well-cared for when you're no longer able to provide that care yourself. Step 1: Identify potential guardians Consider friends, family members, or professional pet-sitting services who would be willing and capable of taking care of your pets. Discuss this possibility with them beforehand, ensuring they understand their responsibilities and are comfortable assuming this role. Keep in mind that the chosen guardian(s) should have compatible personalities and lifestyles with your pets. Key tip: It's wise to name alternate guardians as well, in case your first choice becomes unable to fulfill their duties. Step 2: Determine financial arrangements Decide how pet-related expenses will be covered by the designated guardian(s). You may choose to set aside funds specifically for this purpose, establish a trust, or include provisions in your will. Be sure to discuss these details with both the guardian(s) and any relevant professionals (such as attorneys or financial advisors). Guideline: When estimating costs, consider food, grooming, veterinary care, medications, and other necessary supplies or services. Step 3: Consult legal counsel While it's possible to create a basic pet protection agreement without legal assistance, consulting an attorney experienced in estate planning or animal law can help ensure that your document is legally binding and comprehensive. They can also offer guidance on specific state laws regarding pet ownership and inheritance. Key tip: If using an attorney isn't feasible due to cost constraints, consider utilizing free or low-cost legal clinics offered by local bar associations or universities. Step 4: Outline the terms of the agreement Begin by clearly stating the names and descriptions of all pets involved, including breed, age, sex, color, and any distinguishing features. Then, detail the agreed-upon care arrangements, including living conditions, feeding schedules, exercise routines, medical needs, and socialization requirements. Guideline: To avoid confusion, use precise language and be as specific as possible when describing the expected level of care. Step 5: Address contingencies In addition to naming primary and secondary guardians, account for unexpected situations by addressing scenarios like relocation, unforeseen circumstances affecting the guardian's ability to care for the pet, and end-of-life decisions. Including these provisions ensures that your pets receive consistent care regardless of external factors. Key tip: Consider consulting with veterinarians or animal welfare organizations to gain insight into best practices for handling various contingencies. Step 6: Execute the agreement Once the pet protection agreement has been drafted, review it carefully with all parties involved. Make any necessary revisions based on feedback, then sign and date the final version. Provide copies to all relevant parties, including guardians, alternates, and veterinarians. Guideline: It's recommended to periodically review and update the agreement as needed, especially if there are changes in circumstance or legislation related to pet ownership. By following this comprehensive guide, you can craft a thorough and effective pet protection agreement that provides peace of mind for both you and your cherished companions. Step 1: Understand What Purple Prose Is Purple prose is writing that uses an excess of adjectives, elaborate metaphors, and flowery language to describe people, places, or things. It draws attention to itself rather than enhancing the story or message being conveyed. By understanding what purple prose is, you can learn to identify and avoid using this type of overly-ornate language in your own writing. Step 2: Recognize the Signs of Purple Prose Some common signs of purple prose include: * Overuse of adverbs and adjectives: When every noun has multiple descriptors or verbs are constantly modified with -ly words, it creates wordiness and detracts from the impact of the writing. * Excessive use of figurative language: While similes, metaphors, and other forms of figurative language can enrich your writing, too much of it becomes distracting and dilutes the meaning of your text. * Unnecessary complexity: If your sentences contain many clauses and convoluted structures, they may be difficult to follow and understand, which hinders communication. Step 3: Analyze Your Own Writing for Purple Prose To determine if your writing contains instances of purple prose, read it aloud slowly and consider whether any phrases seem unnecessarily complicated or ornate. Additionally, ask yourself these questions: * Does the description enhance the narrative or slow down the pacing? * Are there more concise ways to express the same ideas without losing their essence? * Would removing some adjectives or adverbs make the sentence clearer and more direct? If you answer 'yes' to any of these questions, consider revising those sections to remove elements of purple prose. Step 4: Edit Ruthlessly When editing your work, focus on cutting unnecessary words and simplifying complex sentences. Look for opportunities to replace lengthy descriptions with stronger, more precise language. Remember that sometimes less is more - stripping away excessive detail allows the reader to focus on the most important aspects of your writing. Step 5: Practice Concision and Clarity Develop habits that promote concise and clear writing by following these guidelines: * Limit adverb usage: Instead of relying on adverbs to modify verbs, choose strong action words that accurately convey the intended meaning. * Use active voice: Sentences written in active voice generally require fewer words and create more engaging prose. * Vary sentence structure: Mix short and long sentences to maintain rhythm and interest while avoiding monotony. * Eliminate redundancies: Avoid repeating information or using synonymous terms close together; trust your reader to understand the concepts presented. Step 6: Seek Feedback From Others Share your writing with others and solicit feedback about potential instances of purple prose. A fresh perspective can help you identify areas where your writing might benefit from greater simplicity and clarity. Be open to constructive criticism and use it as an opportunity to improve your craft. By following these steps, you will develop skills that enable you to recognize and eliminate purple prose from your writing. This will result in stronger, more effective prose that communicates your ideas clearly and engagingly. " The itsy-bitsy spider went up the water spout down came the rain." From Seed to Pumpkin is a great story that will answer kids questions about where pumpkins come from and it integrates science with a seasonal theme. The story begins with the farmer planting seeds in the spring and takes you through an entire year of the life of a pumpkin. Throughout the story there are explanations describing how the seeds are growing and what the needs of the plant are. It explains how flowers bloom on the vines and after they wither away they turn into tiny fruits that begin to grow. The book also explains some uses of pumpkins as jack-o'-lanterns and for pumpkin pie for fall holidays. At the end of the story it is spring again and the farmer is out planting more pumpkin seeds. This book will give children a great understanding of the needs of plants and how they grow throughout the seasons of the year. This book is a great tool to use for a unit on plants and living things, pumpkins or Halloween. It discusses the needs of plants(air, water, light, place to grow) and the parts of the plant(seed, stem, roots, leaves, stem, flower buds). It has ideas for bulletin boards, crafts, and even some pumpkin poems. - The Pumpkin Circle has a great informational page that discusses lots of questions about pumpkins from how to grow then to when should they be picked. - Education World has a good lesson for predictions with pumpkins where kids get to count the seeds of different size pumpkins and then graph the results. Title: How to Draw Manga Anime Face Introduction: Drawing manga and anime faces can be a fun and rewarding experience. With practice and patience, anyone can learn to draw these distinctive, stylized characters. This tutorial will guide you through the process of drawing a basic manga anime face, focusing on clear instructions and helpful tips. Step 1: Understand Basic Facial Proportions Before diving into the specifics of manga and anime styles, familiarize yourself with general facial proportions. A common guideline is to divide the head vertically into five equal sections and horizontally into three equal parts. The vertical divisions help align features like eyes, nose, and mouth, while horizontal lines indicate where those features typically fall within the head. Key Tip: Remember that these are just rough guides-individual faces may vary significantly from this standard layout. Step 2: Outline the Head Shape Manga and anime heads often have large, round shapes. Start by sketching an oval or circle as your base shape. Then, add two curved lines at the bottom to create a slight chin indentation. Be sure to maintain symmetry between left and right sides of the face. Step 3: Position the Eyes The eyes usually sit about halfway down the head, spanning across the second and third horizontal divisions. For most characters, place them closer to the top of the head rather than centered. Sketch two almond-shaped ovals for the eyes, ensuring they're level with one another. Key Guideline: Keep in mind that different character types might require adjusted eye placement (e.g., childlike characters may have larger eyes placed higher up). Step 4: Add Eye Details Divide each eye into three parts-top, iris, and bottom. The iris should take up around half of the total eye height. Above the iris, draw a small curve representing eyelashes or eyebrows; below it, leave some space before adding a thin line for the lower lash line. Step 5: Create Nostrils and a Smile Line Approximately one fifth of the way down from the eyes, mark two small dots for nostrils. Just above these, lightly sketch a short, slightly curved line to suggest a smile or neutral expression. Avoid placing this line too high or low, as it could imply a different emotion. Step 6: Form Lips and Earlobes Now, refine the earlier smile line into full lips. Curve the upper lip gently downward from the smile line and then back upwards to meet the lower lip. Extend subtle curves outwards from the corners of the mouth to represent earlobes. Step 7: Design Hair and Hairstyle Hair is a crucial aspect of manga and anime faces. Begin by roughly blocking out major hair sections using simple shapes like triangles, rectangles, or circles. Gradually build upon these foundational forms until you achieve the desired hairstyle. Key Tip: When designing hair, consider factors such as length, texture, volume, and partings to convey personality traits and further distinguish your character. Step 8: Final Touches and Refinement Lastly, revisit all drawn elements to ensure accuracy and consistency. Correct any misaligned features, smooth out jagged edges, and enhance details if necessary. Don't forget to erase unnecessary construction lines once satisfied with the result. Conclusion: Practice regularly to improve your skills in drawing manga and anime faces. Experiment with various expressions, poses, and stylistic choices to develop unique characters. Most importantly, enjoy the creative process! In later versions, a touch screen works as well. However, using both a keyboard and a mouse makes many tasks easier. By clicking a few buttons on the screen, Windows helps keep your files safe, and easier to change and move. Versions of Windows after 2005 make it even easier for some users with disabilities because these versions have touch screens. For use of a touch screen, some mobile devices come with Windows. From: Allison from No Time for Flash Cards shared this great way to teach your tot about rhyming words. - A list of 5 simple words plus 4 or 5 words that rhyme with each of them. Here are some options: - bug: hug, jug, mug, plug, rug, drug, slug, smug, tug - cat: bat, chat, fat, flat, gnat, hat, mat, pat, rat, sat, scat, splat, that, vat - dog: hog, log, bog, frog, jog, smog, - pig: big, dig, fig, jig, sprig, swig, twig, wig - sun: bun, done, fun, none, nun, one, pun, spun, stun - About 25 craft sticks - Thin lined permanent marker (I love the new Mark It ones from Bic. They come in lots of colors and write on almost everything.) - 4-5 Small cups. - Painter's masking tape. - Basket or bowl to hold the rhyming flags. - Place a piece of tape on each cup. - Use the marker to write the first word in each rhyming set on it's own cup. - Lay out a long strip of masking tape, face up on a table. - Place the craft sticks about 2 inches apart along the tape. - Place a second piece of tape directly over the first piece, "sealing" the craft sticks between them. - Cut the tape to separate the craft sticks and create a tape "flag" on each one. - Clearly print one word on the front (and back) of each "flag". - Place all of the craft sticks in a basket. - Remind your tot that rhyming words are words that have the same ending sound. Give a couple of examples ("cat" and "bat") and encourage your tot to think of a word that rhymes with them ("I can think of something that rhymes with "cat". You put it on your head ") - Explain that you are going to place the flag sticks in the cup that has the correct rhyming word. - Read the word on each cup out loud. - Invite your child to select a flag stick from the basket. - Either have your child read the word on the flag, or read it for them. - Ask you child to place the flag stick in the cup that contains the word that rhymes. - If your tot needs more help, say the word on the flag and then repeat the words on each of the cups again. For example, if your tot selects a stick with the word "hog" on it, you could point to the first cup and say "This says "cat". Does 'hog -og -og -og' have the same ending sound as 'cat -at -at -at' "? - Repeat the procedure for all of the rest of the sticks in the basket. Observations: Sweet Pea had a hard time at first and I had to help her a lot. She started catching on after 4 or 5 sticks and did seem to like it, but only placed about half of the flag sticks before getting silly. I have noticed that Sweet Pea has a hard time coming up with rhyming words (one of the reasons that I introduced this activity) and I think this activity was just a tiny bit too difficult for her. We started off well, but it just didn't quite hold Sweet Pea's interest. I suspect that she will like it better in another few months. I intentionally added some "tricky" rhyming words such as "none" to put in the "sun" cup and Sweet Pea found those impossible without my help. I think that would be a good idea for an older child who is a little more comfortable with rhyming, but I wish I wouldn't have done it this time. Notes from the Trenches: We used small plastic Dixie cups which were okay, but they fell over a lot. Heavier cups would probably work better. Dressing up as May from Pok mon can be an exciting way to show your love for the beloved character from the popular anime and video game series. This tutorial will provide you with a comprehensive guide on achieving an accurate and impressive May costume. ### Step 1: Gather materials and supplies Before starting, ensure that you have all necessary items for this project. Here is a list of essentials to create a May outfit: * A short-sleeved white blouse or shirt * A red mini skirt or a pair of red shorts * A black choker necklace * A brown waist belt * White knee-high socks or tall boots * A blue bandana or scarf * Yellow wristbands (optional) * Blue contacts (optional) Please note that some accessories like yellow wristbands and blue contact lenses are optional but would add more accuracy to your cosplay. You may also want to consider purchasing a wig if your hair color or length doesn't match May's appearance. ### Step 2: Prepare the base clothing layers Start by wearing the white short-sleeved blouse or shirt tucked into the red mini skirt or shorts. Make sure the collar is neatly pressed down and avoid any visible wrinkles on both garments. For bottom wear, choose between a mini skirt or shorts depending on personal comfort and appropriateness. ### Step 3: Add the choker necklace Put on the black choker necklace snuggly around your neck. Chokers were quite fashionable during May's era, so make sure it fits well without being too tight. ### Step 4: Secure the waist belt Fasten the brown leather waist belt around your hips over the blouse and skirt/shorts. Position it slightly higher than where you usually wear belts to mimic May's look accurately. ### Step 5: Style the legwear Opt for either white knee-high socks or tall boots. If using socks, pull them up to just below your knees. Alternatively, if you prefer boots, select ones that resemble her design - they should ideally be mid-calf height, flat soles, and dark colored. ### Step 6: Accessorize with a bandana/scarf Take the blue bandana or scarf and tie it around your left thigh, securing it with knots at both ends. This is one of May's most distinctive features, signifying her status as a coordinator. ### Step 7: Consider additional details If desired, include yellow wristbands on both arms above your gloves. These aren't always noticeable due to long sleeves, but could enhance authenticity. Additionally, if possible, obtain blue contact lenses to truly capture May's eye color. ### Step 8: Hairstyle & makeup May has shoulder-length orange hair typically styled in pigtails tied with red ribbons. To recreate this hairstyle, you might need a wig unless your natural hair matches these specifications. Part your hair down the middle then gather two sections near your ears and secure each section with a red ribbon. As for makeup, keep it simple yet polished. Focus on maintaining clean skin, emphasizing your eyes with mascara and eyeliner, and applying lip balm or gloss. Remember, less is often more when aiming for screen accuracy! By following these steps meticulously, you'll successfully transform into May from Pok mon. Title: How to Perform Razor Scooter Trickes Introduction: Razor scooters are not just for commuting; they can also be used to perform exciting tricks. With practice and patience, you can master various stunts that will make your rides more thrilling and enjoyable. This article will provide an in-depth guide on performing some popular razor scooter tricks. Remember always to wear safety gear such as helmets, knee pads, and elbow pads while practicing these tricks. 1. The Bunny Hop Level: Beginner Step 1: Start by standing on your razor scooter with both feet shoulder-width apart over the back wheels. Make sure your dominant foot is at the rear. * Positioning yourself correctly allows better control during the trick. Step 2: Crouch down slightly, bend your knees, and prepare to jump. Keep your balance centered over the scooter. * Good balance ensures stability when lifting off the ground. Step 3: Swiftly push down on the handlebars using your arms' strength while simultaneously jumping upwards with your legs. As you leap, pull the scooter upwards so it rotates 90 degrees anti-clockwise if you're goofy-footed (dominant right foot) or clockwise if regular-footed (dominant left foot). * Applying force to the handlebars assists in lifting the front wheel off the ground, while pushing up with your legs raises the back end. Step 4: While still mid-air, turn the handlebars back straight and land gently on all four wheels. Absorb the impact through bent knees to avoid injury. Key Tips: * Practice the crouching and jumping motion without the scooter first until you feel comfortable. * Gradually increase the height of your hops once you get confident. 2. The Manual Level: Intermediate Title: How to Flip an Image in Google Docs Google Docs is a powerful online word processing tool that allows users to create, edit, and share documents. One feature that Google Docs offers is the ability to insert and manipulate images within your document. This includes flipping an image horizontally or vertically. Here's a detailed guide on how to flip an image in Google Docs: 1. Open your Google Docs account and open the document where you want to flip an image. Key tip: Make sure you have saved any changes before proceeding to avoid losing unsaved work. 2. Click on the location within the document where you would like the flipped image to appear. Guideline: Placing the cursor at the correct position ensures proper placement of the image once inserted. 3. Select 'Insert' from the top menu bar, then hover over 'Image'. Choose one of three options - Upload from computer, Drive, or URL - depending on where the original image is stored. In-depth explanation: The 'Upload from computer' option lets you select an image file from your device; 'Drive' enables choosing an image previously uploaded to your Google Drive; while 'URL' provides an input field to enter the web address of the image. 4. Once you've selected the source, navigate through your files (or webpage) to locate the image you wish to flip. After finding it, click 'Open' or 'Select', depending on whether you are using a dialog box or a dropdown list. 5. With the image now placed into your document, it's time to flip it. First, ensure the image is selected by clicking on it. You will know it's selected when arrows appear around its edges. 6. Next, look for the 'Format options' pane on the right side of the screen if it isn't already visible. If it says 'Show format options' instead, click there to reveal the pane. 7. Within the 'Format options' pane, find and click on the 'Image properties' tab near the top. A submenu should expand below it. 8. Scroll down until you see two rotation buttons under the 'Adjustments' section - one with a curved arrow pointing left and another with a curved arrow pointing upwards. These are the horizontal and vertical flip buttons respectively. 9. To perform a horizontal flip, click the button depicting a curved arrow pointing left. Your image should immediately reverse along its horizontal axis, creating a mirror effect. Key Tip: Remember, this action cannot be undone via Ctrl+Z shortcut since changing view doesn't affect text flow or layout. Always make copies of important images before editing them. 10. For performing a vertical flip, simply hit the button showing a curved arrow aiming upward. Consequently, your image will reflect along its vertical axis, making it appear upside-down. Remember, these steps allow you to enhance your document aesthetics without needing external software. Practice regularly till you get comfortable with Google Docs features!
