Title: How to Add FarmVille 2 Neighbors Without Adding Them on Facebook Introduction: FarmVille 2 is a popular farming simulation game that allows players to build their own dream farm while interacting with friends and neighbors. One common issue faced by many players is adding neighbors without having to add them as friends on Facebook. This tutorial will provide you with an easy-to-follow guide on how to do just that! By following these steps, you'll be able to expand your farming community without cluttering up your personal social media space. **Note**: While this method does not require you to add people as friends on Facebook, you still need a valid Facebook account linked to your FarmVille 2 game. Step 1: Access Your Current Friends List 1. Launch FarmVille 2 through your preferred platform (Facebook, Windows, iOS or Android). 2. Log into your Facebook account if necessary. 3. Click on the 'Friends' icon located at the bottom left corner of the screen (iOS/Android) or upper right corner near the gear icon (web version). 4. Select 'Neighbor Plow' from the list. You should now see all your current neighbors in alphabetical order. Key Tip: Make sure you have enough neighbor slots available before proceeding. If needed, remove some existing neighbors who are less active or unresponsive. To do so, click on the 'X' button next to their name under the 'Remove Friend' section. Confirm removal when prompted. Step 2: Find New Potential Neighbors 1. Visit online communities dedicated to FarmVille 2 such as forums, fan pages, or groups. Popular options include Zynga Forums, FarmVille Freak, or FarmVille 2 Official Group. 2. Look out for posts where users request new neighbors or offer themselves as neighbors. These threads usually contain user ID numbers or links to their profiles. Step 3: Send Neighbor Requests 1. Identify potential neighbors based on their provided ID number or profile link. Remember to read any additional requirements they may have, like being active daily or reaching certain levels. 2. On web versions, copy their user ID number. Then go back to your FarmVille 2 game, paste it into the search bar above your friend list, and press enter. Alternatively, open the shared profile link directly within the app (iOS/Android). 3. Once you find the correct user, send them a neighbor request by clicking the 'Add Neighbor' button beside their name. A confirmation message should appear indicating the successful sending of the request. Step 4: Manage Pending Requests 1. Return to your Neighbor Plow menu after sending requests. Refresh the page if necessary to update the status of pending requests. 2. Accept incoming neighbor requests by clicking 'Confirm' next to their names. Be selective about whom you accept; ensure they meet your criteria for activity level and engagement. Guideline: Maintain good etiquette throughout this process. Respond to messages promptly, respect others' decisions regarding accepting/declining requests, and avoid spamming multiple requests in quick succession. Conclusion: Congratulations! You've successfully added new neighbors to FarmVille 2 without needing to add them on Facebook. Enjoy building your virtual farm together while respecting both parties' privacy boundaries. The question given below is a Math problem in Algebra and requires framing a couple of linear equations and solving the equations. A poultry farm has only chickens and pigs. When the manager of the poultry counted the heads of the stock in the farm, the number totaled up to 200. However, when the number of legs was counted, the number totaled up to 540. How many chickens were there in the farm? The correct choice is (D) and the correct answer is 130 Let there by 'x' chickens and 'y' pigs. Therefore, x + y = 200 --- (1) Each chicken has 2 legs and each pig has 4 legs Therefore, 2x + 4y = 540 --- (2) Solving equations (1) and (2), we get x = 130 and y = 70. There were 130 chickens and 70 pigs in the farm. They're not going to go to school when they don't height. [Hat begins to turn the pages again] They were going to the park to have a picnic. Which part of your body lets you read the back of a cereal box, check out a rainbow, and see a softball heading your way? Which part lets you cry when you're sad and makes tears to protect itself? Which part has muscles that adjust to let you focus on things that are close up or far away? If you guessed the eye, you're right! Your eyes are at work from the moment you wake up to the moment you close them to go to sleep. They take in tons of information about the world around you - shapes, colors, movements, and more. Then they send the information to your brain for processing so the brain knows what's going on outside of your body. You can see that the eye's pretty amazing. So, come on - let's take a tour of its many parts. The Parts of the Eye You can check out different parts of the eye by looking at your own eye in the mirror or by looking at (but not touching) a friend's eye. Some of the eye's parts are easy to see, so most friends will say OK. Most friends won't say OK if you ask to see their liver! The eye is about as big as a ping-pong ball and sits in a little hollow area (the eye socket) in the skull. The eyelid protects the front part of the eye. The lid helps keep the eye clean and moist by opening and shutting several times a minute. This is called blinking, and it's both a voluntary and involuntary action, meaning you can blink whenever you want to, but it also happens without you even thinking about it. The eyelid also has great reflexes, which are automatic body responses, that protect the eye. When you step into bright light, for example, the eyelids squeeze together tightly to protect your eyes until they can adjust to the light. And if you flutter your fingers close (but not too close!) to your friend's eyes, you'll be sure to see your friend's eyes blink. Your friend's eyelids shut automatically to protect the eye from possible danger. And speaking of fluttering, don't forget eyelashes. They work with the eyelids to keep dirt and other unwanted stuff out of your eyes. The white part of the eyeball is called the sclera (say: SKLAIR-uh). The sclera is made of a tough material and has the important job of covering most of the eyeball. Think of the sclera as your eyeball's outer coat. Look very closely at the white of the eye, and you'll see lines that look like tiny pink threads. These are blood vessels, the tiny tubes that deliver blood, to the sclera. The cornea (say: KOR-nee-uh), a transparent dome, sits in front of the colored part of the eye. The cornea helps the eye focus as light makes its way through. It is a very important part of the eye, but you can hardly see it because it's made of clear tissue. Like clear glass, the cornea gives your eye a clear window to view the world through. Iris Is The Colorful Part Behind the cornea are the iris, the pupil, and the anterior chamber. The iris (say: EYE-riss) is the colorful part of the eye. When we say a person has blue eyes, we really mean the person has blue irises! The iris has muscles attached to it that change its shape. This allows the iris to control how much light goes through the pupil (say: PYOO-pul). The pupil is the black circle in the center of the iris, which is really an opening in the iris, and it lets light enter the eye. To see how this works, use a small flashlight to see how your eyes or a friend's eyes respond to changes in brightness. The pupils will get smaller when the light shines near them and they'll open wider when the light is gone. The anterior (say: AN-teer-ee-ur) chamber is the space between the cornea and the iris. This space is filled with a special transparent fluid that nourishes the eye and keeps it healthy. These next parts are really cool, but you can't see them with just your own eyes! Doctors use special microscopes to look at these inner parts of the eye, such as the lens. After light enters the pupil, it hits the lens. The lens sits behind the iris and is clear and colorless. The lens' job is to focus light rays on the back of the eyeball - a part called the retina (say: RET-i-nuh). The lens works much like the lens of a movie projector at the movies. Next time you sit in the dark theater, look behind you at the stream of light coming from the projection booth. This light goes through a powerful lens, which is focusing the images onto the screen, so you can see the movie clearly. In the eye's case, however, the film screen is your retina. Your retina is in the very back of the eye. It holds millions of cells that are sensitive to light. The retina takes the light the eye receives and changes it into nerve signals so the brain can understand what the eye is seeing. A Muscle Makes It Work The lens is suspended in the eye by a bunch of fibers. These fibers are attached to a muscle called the ciliary (say: SIL-ee-air-ee) muscle. The ciliary muscle has the amazing job of changing the shape of the lens. That's right - the lens actually changes shape right inside your eye! Try looking away from your computer and focusing on something way across the room. Even though you didn't feel a thing, the shape of your lenses changed. When you look at things up close, the lens becomes thicker to focus the correct image onto the retina. When you look at things far away, the lens becomes thinner. The biggest part of the eye sits behind the lens and is called the vitreous (say: VIH-tree-us) body. The vitreous body forms two thirds of the eye's volume and gives the eye its shape. It's filled with a clear, jelly-like material called the vitreous humor. Ever touch toy eyeballs in a store? Sometimes they're kind of squishy - that's because they're made to feel like they're filled with vitreous humor. In a real eye, after light passes through the lens, it shines straight through the vitreous humor to the back of the eye. The retina uses special cells called rods and cones to process light. Just how many rods and cones does your retina have? How about 120 million rods and 7 million cones - in each eye! Rods see in black, white, and shades of gray and tell us the form or shape that something has. Rods can't tell the difference between colors, but they are super-sensitive, allowing us to see when it's very dark. Cones sense color and they need more light than rods to work well. Cones are most helpful in normal or bright light. The retina has three types of cones. Each cone type is sensitive to one of three different colors - red, green, or blue - to help you see different ranges of color. Together, these cones can sense combinations of light waves that enable our eyes to see millions of colors. Helping You See It All Rods and cones process the light to give you the total picture. You're able to see that your friend has brown skin and is wearing a blue hat while he tosses an orange basketball. Sometimes someone's eyeball shape makes it difficult for the cornea, lens, and retina to work perfectly as a team. When this happens, some of what the person sees will be out of focus. To correct this fuzzy vision, many people, including many kids, wear glasses. Glasses help the eyes focus images correctly on the retina and allow someone to see clearly. As adults get older, their eyes lose the ability to focus well and they often need glasses to see things up close or far away. Most older people you know - like your grandparents - probably wear glasses. To the Brain! Think of the optic nerve as the great messenger in the back of your eye. The rods and cones of the retina change the colors and shapes you see into millions of nerve messages. Then, the optic nerve carries those messages from the eye to the brain! The optic nerve serves as a high-speed telephone line connecting the eye to the brain. When you see an image, your eye "telephones" your brain with a report on what you are seeing so the brain can translate that report into "cat," "apple," or "bicycle," or whatever the case may be. For crying out loud, the eye has its own special bathing system - tears! Above the outer corner of each eye are the lacrimal (say: LAK-ruh-mul) glands, which make tears. Every time you blink your eye, a tiny bit of tear fluid comes out of your upper eyelid. It helps wash away germs, dust, or other particles that don't belong in your eye. Tears also keep your eye from drying out. Then the fluid drains out of your eye by going into the lacrimal duct (this is also called the tear duct). You can see the opening of your tear duct if you very gently pull down the inside corner of your eye. When you see a tiny little hole, you've found the tear duct. Your eyes sometimes make more tear fluid than normal to protect themselves. This may have happened to you if you've been poked in the eye, if you've been in a dusty or smoking area, or if you've been near someone who's cutting onions. And how about the last time you felt sad, scared, or upset? Your eyes got a message from your brain to make you cry, and the lacrimal glands made many, many tears. Your eyes do some great things for you, so take these steps to protect them: Wear protective goggles in classes where debris or chemicals could go flying, such as wood shop, metal shop, science lab, or art. Wear eye protection when playing racquetball, hockey, skiing, or other sports that could injure your eyes. Wear sunglasses. Too much light can damage your eyes and cause vision problems, such as cataracts, later in life. If the lens gets cloudy, it's called a cataract. A cataract prevents light from reaching the retina and makes it difficult to see. The eyes you have will be yours forever - treat them right and they'll never be out of sight! In ancient times, people from one city or area would attack another place, and make the people there pay "tribute". Tribute meant that the attacked people would pay money (or other things), and the attacker would stop attacking them. A famous tribute was the Danegeld, when people from Denmark conquered part of England and made the English pay thousands of silver coins. Okay, he's kinda wheezing out a little but... Title: How to Become a Gothic Girl Introduction: The gothic subculture is a complex and diverse community that has been around since the 1980s. It's characterized by its dark, mysterious, and often romantic aesthetic, which draws inspiration from various sources such as literature, music, fashion, and art. If you're interested in exploring this unique subculture and becoming a gothic girl, then read on! This comprehensive guide will provide you with all the necessary information and steps to help you get started. Remember, being a goth is about self-expression and individuality; there are no strict rules or regulations to follow. However, understanding the core elements of the subculture can make your transition smoother and more enjoyable. Step 1: Research the Subculture Before diving headfirst into the goth scene, take some time to research and understand its history, values, and aesthetics. Read books, watch documentaries, listen to gothic music, and engage with online communities to gain insights into what being a goth entails. Some recommended resources include: - Books: "Gothic Charm School" by Jillian Venters and "The Mourning Ring" by Marissa Levien - Documentaries: "Modern Vampires," "New York City's Shadow: The Rise of Goth Culture," and "Visionaries of the Night: A Journey Into The southwest has a climate similar to that of the Mediterranean with mild, moderately wet winters and hot, dry summers. This area also makes much of the wine in South Africa. This region is also mostly known for its wind, which blows almost all year. The wind can sometimes be severe when passing around the Cape of Good Hope mostly gets bad for sailors, making many shipwrecks. Further east on the south coast, rainfall falls more evenly throughout the year, making a green landscape. This area is popularly known as the Garden Route. Title: How to Perform a Front Kick in Kickboxing Introduction: Kickboxing is an exciting and physically demanding sport that combines elements from various martial arts such as Karate, Muay Thai, and Boxing. The front kick is one of the fundamental techniques used in kickboxing, which can be utilized both offensively and defensively. This tutorial will provide you with an in-depth guide on executing a proper front kick in kickboxing, ensuring clarity, practicality, and ease of understanding. Section 1: Stance and Balance A solid stance and balance are crucial when performing any kickboxing technique, especially the front kick. Follow these steps to establish your base: 1. Start by standing in a fighting stance. Your dominant foot should be placed behind (approximately one foot length) and slightly offset from your non-dominant foot. Keep your body weight evenly distributed between both legs. 2. Bend your knees slightly while keeping your back straight. Maintain this position throughout the entire movement to ensure stability and power transfer. 3. Position your hands at face level, protecting yourself from potential punches or strikes. Tuck your chin downwards, maintaining eye contact with your opponent while avoiding exposing your nose or jawline. 4. Engage your core muscles to stabilize your upper body and maintain balance during the execution of the front kick. Section 2: Chambering and Execution Chambering refers to preparing your leg for the kick before making contact with your target. Proper chambering ensures maximum speed, accuracy, and force upon impact. 5. Begin by shifting your bodyweight onto your rear leg without altering your overall stance. Simultaneously raise your kicking leg's knee towards your chest, pointing it directly upwards. This motion chambers the kick, setting the stage for powerful execution. 6. Extend your kicking leg forward, pivoting on the ball of your supporting foot (non-kicking leg). Rotate your hips open, turning them away from the direction of the kick. This rotation adds significant torque and momentum, increasing the effectiveness of the strike. 7. Make contact with the target using the ball of your foot or shin, depending on the distance and height of the intended object. Aim for a firm yet controlled touch rather than attempting to crush through the target. Overextending may compromise your balance and leave you vulnerable. 8. Upon completion of the kick, swiftly retract your leg back into its original chambered position. Return your body weight to an equal distribution over both feet. Finally, reset your hands and return them to their initial defensive positions near your face. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Practice consistently to improve muscle memory, coordination, and precision. * Focus on generating power via hip rotation instead of solely relying on brute strength. * Exhale sharply as you execute the kick; doing so enhances explosiveness and releases tension buildup. * Develop flexibility gradually through stretching exercises to prevent injuries and optimize range of motion. * Consider incorporating resistance bands or partner drills to strengthen lower body musculature further. * Always prioritize safety and practice under professional supervision until mastery is achieved. Okay how long have we been with you guys? Are you going to turn off the recorder? A narrative is a story. When you write a narrative, you are writing about an interesting event and what it means. A narrative can be short or long. The narrative that you are about to write will be about two pages long. ELA Standard 2.1 Before you write a narrative, Come up with a story that you want to share. It can be a story that you made up or it can be based on another story you know. The main thing is to tell the story in a way that is all your own. Jot down ideas! Be sure to answer the following questions: Use a Story Map like the one below to plan your story. Be sure to include the: Title, Setting, Characters, Problem, and Action at the Beginning, Middle, and End of your story. Create extra circles if you need to. Be sure to add details to the setting and characters. Now that you've come up with a plan for your story, it's time to write a first draft! Use your story map to help you remember all the details you want to include. If you can, try to write the whole story all at once. Don't worry about any spelling or grammar mistakes. Since this is a first draft, you don't even need to make sure that your handwriting is neat! Read your narrative over. You might even read it aloud to a friend or teacher. You and your friend or teacher should decide: Rewrite your narrative so that it fully answers these questions. You should also edit your work by checking your spelling and punctuation. Look up any words that you are unsure of in the dictionary. Now copy out the final version of your narrative in your best handwriting or print it out on a computer. Read through your final draft twice. The first time, check that the story is exactly the way you want it. (If it isn't, go back and make more changes.) If you like your final draft, check that there are no spelling or punctuation errors. Be sure to put your name, the date and title on the first page. Now, you can share your story with your friends! Title: How to Change Your Location on Bumble Bumble is a popular dating app that allows you to connect with people nearby. However, if you're traveling or simply looking to meet new people outside your current location, changing your location within the app can be beneficial. This tutorial will guide you through the process of changing your location on Bumble in detail. ### Section 1: Checking Your Current Subscription Status Before proceeding, ensure you have an active subscription to Bumble Boost or Bumble Premium, as these are required to change your location. Here's how to check your subscription status: 1. Open the Bumble app on your device. 2. Tap the profile icon at the bottom right corner of the screen. 3. Select 'Settings.' 4. Scroll down to find 'Manage Membership,' which displays information about your current subscription plan (if any). If you don't see this option, then you do not currently have a paid subscription. Key tip: You cannot change your location without a paid subscription, so consider upgrading before continuing with this tutorial. ### Section 2: Changing Your Location Manually If you have Bumble Boost or Bumble Premium, you can manually update your location using these steps: 1. Follow Steps 1-3 from the previous section to reach your settings menu. 2. Look for 'Location Settings' under the main settings category. 3. Enable the toggle switch next to 'Travel Mode.' 4. Enter your desired city or address into the search bar provided, select the correct match, and tap 'Set Location.' 5. Confirm the action by tapping 'Continue.' 6. Optionally, set a timer for your travel mode duration-the default setting is one week. Keep in mind that while you can extend the time limit multiple times, doing so may eventually require purchasing additional credits. 7. Once complete, press 'Done' to save changes. By following these steps, you've successfully changed your location on Bumble! Now when swiping, users who fit your preferences and reside in your chosen area will appear on your feed instead of those near your physical location. Guidelines & Key Tips: * Be aware that some users might suspect you aren't genuinely local based on conversation topics, mutual connections, etc., potentially leading them to lose interest. Honesty goes a long way in building trust online. * Always double-check the spelling and formatting of your intended destination during entry to avoid errors. * Make sure GPS permissions are enabled for the Bumble app; otherwise, Travel Mode won't function correctly. Remember that changing your location frequently could raise suspicion among other users regarding your authenticity. Use this feature wisely and respectfully towards others. Happy swiping! Disabling built-in video memory can be a useful way to optimize your computer's performance, particularly if you are using an external graphics card or have limited system resources. This guide will walk you through the process of disabling built-in video memory on Windows operating systems. Step 1: Determine if You Need to Disable Built-In Video Memory Before proceeding with this tutorial, it is important to determine whether or not disabling built-in video memory is necessary for your specific situation. If you are experiencing issues with graphical performance on your computer, or if you are trying to conserve system resources, then disabling built-in video memory may be beneficial. However, keep in mind that doing so could potentially impact the overall visual quality of some programs or games. Step 2: Access Device Manager To begin, open the Device Manager by pressing the Windows key + X and selecting "Device Manager" from the menu that appears. Alternatively, you can type "Device Manager" into the search bar on the taskbar and select the corresponding result. Step 3: Locate Display Adapters Once the Device Manager window has opened, expand the category labeled "Display adapters." Here, you should see at least one entry related to your integrated graphics chipset (e.g., Intel HD Graphics). Step 4: Open Properties Menu Right-click on the display adapter you wish to modify and select "Properties" from the context menu that appears. This will open the properties dialog box for the selected device. Step 5: Navigate to Power Management Tab Within the properties dialog box, click on the "Power Management" tab. This tab contains options related to power consumption and resource allocation for the selected display adapter. Step 6: Uncheck Allow Computer to Turn Off This Device to Save Power Locate the checkbox labeled "Allow computer to turn off this device to save power" and uncheck it. By doing so, you are preventing Windows from automatically managing the power state of your integrated graphics chipset. Instead, your computer will always utilize the maximum available video memory for the given device. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Be aware that disabling built-in video memory could negatively affect graphical performance in certain applications or games. It is recommended to test your system after making these changes to ensure compatibility and stability. * Keep in mind that there may be multiple entries within the "Display adapters" section of the Device Manager. Make sure to identify which entry corresponds to your integrated graphics chipset before modifying any settings. * While this tutorial focuses specifically on disabling built-in video memory within Windows operating systems, similar steps can be taken for other platforms such as macOS or Linux distributions. Consult appropriate documentation for guidance tailored to those environments. [tosses one of the shapes to the side] What's the kitty's name over Nana's house? Can I go to the moon asked baby bear? they don't know what's going to happen at the doctor's. Base systems like binary and hexadecimal seem a bit strange at first. The key is understanding how different systems "tick over" like an odometer when they are full. Base 10, our decimal system, "ticks over" when it gets 10 items, creating a new digit. We wait 60 seconds before "ticking over" to a new minute. Hex and binary are similar, but tick over every 16 and 2 items, respectively. Try converting numbers to hex and binary here: Way back when: Unary Numbers Way back in the day, we didn't have base systems! It was uphill both ways, through the snow and blazing heat. When you wanted to count one, you'd write: When you wanted 5, you'd write And clearly, 1 + 5 = 6 l + lllll = llllll This is the simplest way of counting. Enter the Romans In Roman numerals, two was one, twice. Three was one, thrice: one = I two = II three = III However, they decided they could do better than the old tradition of lines in the sand. For five, we could use V to represent lllll and get something like l + V = Vl Not bad, eh? And of course, there are many more symbols (L, C, M, etc.) you can use. The key point is that V and lllll are two ways of encoding the number 5. Give each number a name Another breakthrough was realizing that each number can be its own distinct concept. Rather than represent three as a series of ones, give it its own symbol: "3 . Do this from one to nine, and you get the symbols: 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 9 The Romans were close, so close, but only gave unique symbols to 5, 10, 50, 100, 1000, etc. Use your position Now clearly, you can't give every number its own symbol. There's simply too many But notice one insight about Roman numerals: they use position of symbols to indicate meaning. IV means "subtract 1 from 5 and VI means "add 1 to 5 . In our number system, we use position in a similar way. We always add and never subtract. And each position is 10 more than the one before it. So, 35 means "add 3*10 to 5*1 and 456 means "4*100 + 5*10 + 6*1 . Our choice of base 10 Why did we choose to multiply by 10 each time? Most likely because we have 10 fingers. One point to realize is you need enough digits to "fill up" until you hit the next number. Let me demonstrate. If we want to roll the odometer over every 10, so to speak, we need symbols for numbers one through nine; we haven't reached ten yet. Imagine numbers as ticking slowly upward - at what point do you flip over the next unit and start from nothing? And what happens when we reach ten? How do we show we want exactly one "ten" and nothing in the "ones" column? We use zero, the number that doesn't exist. Zero is quite a concept, it's a placeholder, a blank, a space, and a whole lot more. Suffice it to say, Zero is one of the great inventions of all time. Zero allows us to have an empty placeholder, something the Romans didn't have. Look how unwieldly their numbers are without it. George Orwell's famous novel "1984 would be "MCMLXXXIV"! Rolls right off the tongue, doesn't it? Considering other bases Remember that we chose to roll over our odometer every ten. Our counting looks like this: 9 (uh oh, I'm getting full!) 10 (ticked over - start a new digit) What if we ticked over at 60 when we counted, like we do for seconds and minutes? 1:00 (60 seconds aka 1 minute. We've started a new digit.) Everything OK so far, right? Note that we use the colon (:) indicate that we are at a new "digit". In base 10, each digit can stand on its own. Try Base 16 If we want base 16, we could do something similar: 15 (we're getting full) 1:00 (16 - we've started a new digit) However, we don't want to write hexadecimal numbers with the colon notation (even though we could). We'd rather cook up separate symbols for 10-15 so we can just write numbers like we're used to. We've run out of numbers (1-9 already used, with 0 as a placeholder) so we need some other symbols. We could use some squiggly lines or other shapes, but the convenions is to use letters, Roman style. Just like 5 became V, programmers use letters A-F to get enough digits up to 16. That is, A (10 - we're using the symbol "A") F (15 - uh oh, we're getting full) 10 (16 - we start a new digit) Ahah! Now we can use one digit per "place", and we know that 10 actually means we've "ticked over to 16 once. 20 means we've ticked over to 16 twice (32). 25 means we've ticked over to 16 twice (giving us 32) and gone an extra 5. The total is 32 + 5 = 37. With me so far? This is pretty cool, right? We can count in any system we want. Also notice that base 16 is more "space efficient" in the sense we can write a number like 11 in a single digit: B. Base 16 really isn't that different from base 10, we just take longer to fill up. The wonderful world of binary We've seen plenty of base systems, from over-simple unary, to the unweidly Roman numerals, the steady-going base 10 and the compact base 16. What's great about binary? In the spirit of keeping things simple, it's the simplest number system that has the concept of "ticking over". Unary, where we just write 1, 11, 111 just goes on forever. Binary, with two options (1 and 0) looks like this: 2: 10 (we're full - tick over) 4: 100 (we're full again - tick over) 8: 1000 (tick over again) and so on. Because binary is so simple, it's very easy to build in hardware. You just need things that can turn on or off (representing 1 and 0), rather than things that have 10 possible states (to represent decimal). Because it's so simple, binary is also resistant to errors. If your signal is "partially on" (let's say 0.4), you can assume that's a zero. And if it's mostly on (say 0.8), then you can assume it's a 1. If you're using a system with 10 possible states, it's difficult to tell when an error occurred. This is one reason digital signals are so resilient to noise. Other examples of bases We use other bases all the time, even dynamically changing bases. We usually don't think of it that way: Hours, minutes, seconds: 1:32:04 - We know this is 1 hour, 32 minutes, 4 seconds. In seconds, this is 1*60*60 + 32*60 + 4. Feet and inches: 3' 5 - This is 3 feet, 5 in or 3 * 12 + 5 inches. Pounds and ounces: 8 lbs, 5 oz - Since a pound is 16 oz, This is 8 * 16 + 5 oz. We've been using a base 16 number system all along! "10 in any number system indicates the base, and means we've ticked over once. 10 in binary means two, 10 in decimal means ten, and 10 in hexadecimal is sixteen. How do you keep these numbers apart? Programmers will often write "0b" in front of binary numbers. So 2 in binary is Similarly, they'll write 0x in front of hex numbers. So 16 in hex is: If there aren't any symbols (0b or 0x) in front, we assume it's base 10, a regular number. Now go forth and enjoy your new knowledge! This picture is of the Andes Mountains between Chile and Argentina in South America. It was taken from the International Space Station. Do you see the glaciers and snow on top of the mountains? Do you see the lakes in the valleys between mountains? Click on image for full size NASA Earth Observatory This picture was taken from high above our planet. Looking at the Earth from very far away like this we can see that some parts of our planet look light in color, and some parts look dark. The color of these parts affects what happens when sunshine hit them. If the Sun's rays hit a dark part, like an ocean or forest, most of the rays are absorbed. Very few are reflected back out to space. If the Sun's rays hit a light colored part of the Earth's surface, like snow or ice, most of the rays are reflected back out to space. Only a few rays are absorbed. The amount of the Sun's energy that is reflected is called albedo. Dark colored things like oceans and forests have low albedo. Light colored things like snow and ice have very high albedo. About two out of three of the Sun's rays that get to Earth are reflected out to space and the other one is absorbed. The albedo of each planet or moon depends on what is at its surface. Some planets are so far away that they are difficult to see with telescopes. But scientists can figure out what the planet is made of if they can measure its albedo. Earth's climate depends on how many of the Sun's rays are reflected back out to space and how many are absorbed. As our planet gets warmer, more snow and ice melt. Fewer of the Sun's rays are reflected out to space. More are absorbed. This is causing even more warming. Clouds affect albedo too. They have a high albedo and reflect solar energy out to space. If there were no clouds, Earth's average albedo would drop by half. Shop Windows to the Universe Science Store! Learn about Earth and space science, and have fun while doing it! I bet you don't know how to make a bigger bow and arrow. What are you going to do with XXX. Choosing coins for magic tricks is an essential part of being a successful magician. The right coins can make all the difference in executing impressive and mystifying illusions. This guide will walk you through the process of selecting the perfect coins for your magic act. **Step 1: Determine the Type of Trick** The first step in choosing coins for magic tricks is understanding what kind of trick you want to perform. Are you looking to do coin vanishes, productions, transpositions, or penetration tricks? Each type of trick may require different coins with specific characteristics. For example, a coin used for vanishing acts should be easy to handle and able to fit inside small spaces, while a coin used for production tricks might need to have a hollow center for easy loading. **Step 2: Consider Size and Weight** Size and weight are crucial factors when choosing coins for magic tricks. Larger coins tend to be easier to manipulate visually, making them ideal for beginners. Heavier coins also provide better balance and control during sleight-of-hand movements. However, they could potentially draw more attention due to their noise. Therefore, depending on the trick, lighter coins might be preferable if discretion is necessary. Remember that the size and weight should feel comfortable in your hands; otherwise, it may affect your performance negatively. **Step 3: Examine Material and Finish** Coins made from solid metal like copper, brass, silver, or gold offer durability and authenticity. Copper and brass are often chosen for their vintage appeal, whereas silver and gold convey luxury and sophistication. When examining finish, matte finishes reduce glare under stage lights and hide fingerprints well, whereas shiny finishes can add visual interest but require frequent cleaning. Some magicians even choose coins with unique designs or historical significance to enhance the storytelling aspect of their performances. **Step 4: Test Handling and Manipulation** Regardless of how aesthetically pleasing a coin is, its usability is paramount. Before purchasing any coin, try handling and manipulating it. Check whether it spins smoothly between fingers, falls naturally into palm-ups and palms-downs, and stays securely stacked when required. A good magic coin should facilitate these actions effortlessly without causing fatigue over extended periods. **Step 5: Match Your Style and Character** Your choice of coins should align with your character and style as a magician. If you're going for a classic, timeless aesthetic, then antique-looking coins would suit you best. On the other hand, modern tricks might call for contemporary designs. Also, consider whether you want your props to match - having a consistent look across all your magical tools can help establish a cohesive image. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Always prioritize functionality over appearance. No matter how beautiful a coin looks, if it doesn't serve its purpose effectively, it won't benefit your act. * Practice regularly once you've chosen your coins. Familiarize yourself with their dimensions, weight distribution, and overall feel to ensure seamless integration into your routine. * Don't shy away from custom-made coins. Many online platforms specialize in creating personalized magic coins based on individual preferences. * Experiment with various types until you find what works best for you. There's no one-size-fits-all solution when it comes to choosing coins for magic tricks. By following this comprehensive guide, you'll be well equipped to select the most suitable coins for your magic tricks, enhancing both your technique and presentation. Liberia is a country in Southwest Africa, on the coast of the Atlantic Ocean. Except for the coast, most of Liberia is low mountains, with an altitude of to above sea level. The coastal region is full of swamps, and reaches to inland. After that, there's a plateau at an altitude of about . About 60% of the country is covered with rainforest. To the north, there are higher mountains. There are nine big plantations of rubber trees, which are important for the economy. There are mangrove swamps near the coast. In 2019, estimates are that close to 5 million people live in Liberia. About half of them live in the capital, Monrovia. There are 15 administrative divisions, called counties. Bubbles (born 1983) is a common chimpanzee, known for being the one-time pet of American recording artist Michael Jackson. Jackson bought the animal from a Texas research facility in the 1980s. The animal was a frequent travel companion to the singer, whose attachment to the animal led to media mockery and, among other factors, to a public perception of Jackson as an eccentric. Use the digits in the year 2006 and the operations +, -, x, , sqrt (square root), ^ (raise to a power), and ! (factorial), along with grouping symbols, to write expressions for the counting numbers 1 through 100. - All four digits must be used in the expression. - Only the digits 2, 0, 0, 6 may be used. - Double-digit numbers such as 20 or .02 MAY be used this year. - The square function may NOT be used. - The integer function may NOT be used. - Students should be introduced to the fact that 0! by definition is equal to 1. See Dr. Math's answer to Why factorial equal 1? for more information. Dr. Math also tells us that, depending on the context where 0^0 occurs, it can be 1, indeterminate, or undefined/nonexistent. For this game we will accept the value 0^0=1. See the Dr. Math FAQ the 0 power for more information. - This site is intended for the posting of answers generated by students. Teachers may submit answers for their students. - Each answer should include the expression, the name of the student, the student's grade level, and the name of the school. - Use the Web form once for each answer you would like to contribute. If you send more than one solution per form submission, we will be unable to include your answers on our solution page. Title: How to Enable JavaScript in Internet Explorer Introduction: JavaScript is an essential component of many modern websites, enabling interactive features such as forms, animations, and dynamic content loading. If you're using Internet Explorer (IE) and find that certain sites aren't functioning properly or displaying correctly, it may be due to disabled JavaScript. This tutorial will guide you through the process of enabling JavaScript in IE, ensuring optimal functionality when browsing your favorite webpages. Step 1: Launch Internet Explorer Start by opening your existing installation of Internet Explorer on your computer. You can do this quickly by searching for "Internet Explorer" in the Windows Start Menu or by clicking its icon if available on your desktop or taskbar. Key Tip: Make sure you have administrative privileges before making changes to any browser settings. Step 2: Access Internet Options Once Internet Explorer has launched, click the gear-shaped Settings button located at the top right corner of the window. From the dropdown menu, select "Internet Options." This action opens the Internet Properties dialog box containing several tabs with various configuration options for customizing your browsing experience. The Security tab allows controlling access permissions for different website zones while Privacy restricts or enables specific functions based on privacy concerns. However, we need to navigate to another tab to enable JavaScript. Step 3: Navigate to the Security Tab Inside the Internet Properties dialog box, locate and click on the "Security" tab near the top. Here, four distinct security levels are presented - Internet, Local intranet, Trusted Sites, and Restricted Sites. These categories represent where visited websites fall under based on their origin and level of trustworthiness. By default, most users browse within the 'Internet' zone. We shall proceed with configuring our desired setting here. Guideline: Changing settings in lower trusted zones could potentially expose sensitive data; therefore stick to modifying only necessary configurations within the 'Internet' zone unless absolutely required. Step 4: Customize Level Settings Within the Security tab, ensure the 'Internet' option remains selected, then click on the "Custom level..." button below the list of security levels. A new dialog box titled "Security Settings - Internet Zone" appears, filled with numerous checkboxes representing various aspects of IE behavior. Scroll down until you reach the Scripting section. Step 5: Activate Active Scripting Under the Scripting heading, look for the "Active Scripting" entry. It should initially read "Disable," which indicates JavaScript is currently turned off. Click on the radio button next to "Enable" to switch from Disabled to Enabled state. Afterward, hit "Apply" followed by "OK" to save the change and close the opened windows. By activating Active Scripting, you essentially allow execution of JavaScript code embedded within websites, thereby enhancing overall user experience but slightly increasing potential vulnerabilities associated with running scripts. Step 6: Confirm Functionality To verify whether JavaScript is now active, visit a site known for utilizing JavaScript functionalities like Google Maps or YouTube. Upon successful activation, these pages should load without issues related to missing interactivity or non-functional elements. Conclusion: Enabling JavaScript in Internet Explorer unlocks additional layers of interaction and dynamism present on today's complex websites. While doing so introduces minor security risks, following recommended practices such as keeping antivirus software updated significantly mitigates those threats. Always exercise caution when visiting unfamiliar websites and remember to disable scripting again if experiencing persistent problems caused by malicious scripts. Creating an Exercise Music Playlist Step 1: Determine Your Exercise Type Before you start creating your playlist, determine what type of exercise you will be doing. The genre, tempo, and duration of your music should match the intensity and length of your workout. For example, fast-paced music with a high beat per minute (BPM) is ideal for cardio exercises like running or cycling, while slower tempo music can be more suitable for weight lifting or yoga. Key Tips: * Research shows that listening to music during exercise can increase endurance, improve mood, and reduce perceived effort. * Aim for a mix of new and familiar songs to keep your playlist fresh yet comfortable. Step 2: Choose Your Platform Select a platform where you will create and store your playlist. Popular options include Spotify, Apple Music, YouTube, and Pandora. Each platform offers different features, such as curated playlists, personalized radio stations, or access to millions of songs. Guidelines: * Consider factors like cost, user interface, and compatibility with your devices when choosing a platform. * Some platforms offer free trials, so take advantage of these if you're unsure which service to choose. Step 3: Set a Goal Duration Decide on the total length of your playlist based on the estimated duration of your workout. This ensures that you have enough music to last throughout your session without having to constantly skip or change songs. Key Tips: * Add approximately 5-10% extra time to account for delays, rest periods, or variations in pace. * If you're using a streaming service, consider shuffle mode to randomize song order and prevent boredom. Step 4: Select Songs Based on Tempo Choose songs with tempos that align with the intensity of your workout. Use online tools like Songbpm.com to find the BPM of individual tracks. Guidelines: * For warm-up and cool-down phases, opt for lower BPMs (80-100). * During moderate-intensity workouts, aim for mid-range BPMs (110-130). * High-intensity activities benefit from higher BPMs (140+). Step 5: Organize Your Playlist Arrange your chosen songs in an order that progresses naturally with your workout. Generally, this means starting at a lower intensity and gradually increasing as you go along. However, feel free to adjust this structure according to your preferences and specific needs. Key Tips: * Place motivational or upbeat tracks towards the middle or end of your playlist to boost energy levels when they tend to dip. * Create sections within your playlist for variety, such as separate blocks for warming up, stretching, and cooling down. Step 6: Test Your Playlist Exercise alongside your newly created playlist to ensure its effectiveness and cohesion. Pay attention to how well the music matches your movements and energy levels, making any necessary adjustments along the way. Guidelines: * Make note of transitions between songs - ideally, there should be no abrupt changes in tempo or volume. * Be open to removing or adding songs based on their impact during actual exercise. By following these steps and incorporating key tips and guidelines, you'll create an effective exercise music playlist tailored specifically to your fitness goals and preferences. Enjoy the benefits of improved motivation, enhanced performance, and a more enjoyable workout experience! Oh, you don't want to put it in your mouth. Step 1: Determine Your Budget Before choosing a Hawaiian island vacation spot, you need to determine your budget. This is because different islands have varying costs of living, accommodations, food, and activities that can significantly affect your overall expenses. For instance, Maui tends to be more expensive than Oahu or Kauai due to its popularity among tourists. By setting a clear budget, you can narrow down your options based on affordability. Step 2: Identify Your Preferred Activities Each Hawaiian island offers unique experiences and attractions. Knowing what kind of activities you enjoy will help you pick an island that caters best to your interests. Here are some examples: - If you love surfing, windsurfing, or kitesurfing, consider visiting Maui or Oahu which are known for their excellent waves. - If hiking and nature exploration appeal to you, opt for Kauai or Hawaii (Big) Island with their lush landscapes and diverse terrain. - For those interested in history and culture, visit Molokai or Lanai, where traditional Hawaiian life remains relatively untouched by modern development. Step 3: Assess The Type Of Accommodation You Want The type of accommodation you prefer also plays a role in selecting the right island. Luxury resorts are abundant in Maui and Oahu, while eco-friendly cabins and cottages are common in Kauai and Big Island. Consider whether you're looking for a romantic getaway, family-friendly resort, budget hostel, or private rental home. Step 4: Evaluate Travel Time And Convenience Consider how easy it is to reach your chosen destination from your departure city. Direct flights may be available only to certain islands like Honolulu (Oahu), Kahului (Maui), Lihue (Kauai), and Hilo/Kona (Hawaii). Longer travel times might increase fatigue and add to the total cost of the trip. Also, think about if you want to stay on one island throughout your vacation or hop between several. Inter-island travel involves additional time, expense, and planning. Step 5: Research Weather Patterns While all the main Hawaiian Islands share similar tropical climates, they do experience slightly varied weather patterns. For example, the northern shores of Kauai and eastern coast of Big Island receive more rainfall compared to other parts of these islands. Similarly, Haleakala volcano in Maui has cooler temperatures year-round. Understanding these variations can assist you in packing appropriately and deciding which sights to prioritize. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Avoid peak tourist seasons (December - April) to save money and avoid crowds. - Always check reviews before booking any lodging or tour operators. - Respect local customs and traditions during your visit. - Be mindful of environmental impact; choose sustainable practices whenever possible. By following this comprehensive guide, you should be able to select a Hawaiian island vacation spot tailored to your preferences, budget, and needs. Aloha! After the Japanese occupation in the 1940s, the Vietnamese fought French colonial rule during the First Indochina War between the Viet Minh and the French in 2 September 1945. H Ch Minh declared Vietnam's independence from France under the new name of the Democratic Republic of Vietnam, but was fought by French colonialists. In 1954, the Vietnamese declared victory in Dien Bien Phu which took place between March and May 1954 and culminated in a major French defeat. Then Vietnam was divided into two political states, North Vietnam (officially the Democratic Republic of Vietnam) and South Vietnam (officially the Republic of Vietnam). Conflicts between the two sides intensified in the so-called Vietnam War with strong influence from the US in South Vietnam. The war ended in 1975 with a North Vietnamese victory. This is the original page for the slope-intercept form for the equation of a line. All this information plus more can be found on our new slope-intercept form page. You can find that page by clicking here. There is a link back to this original page on the new page. y = mx + b Above is a program that will help you visualize how changing the values for the slope, m, and the y-intercept, b, will affect the graph of the equation y = mx + b. At first the program will be automatically cycling through several values for m and b. If you want to use the sliders to control it yourself, just press the 'You Control' button. Notice that when the slope, m, is positive, the line slants upward to the right. The more positive m is, the steeper the line will slant upward to the right. When the slope is negative, the line slants downward to the right, and, as the slope becomes more and more negative, the line will slant downward steeper and steeper to the right. Also, notice that when the y-intercept, b, is positive, the line crosses the y-axes above y = 0. When b is negative, the line crosses the y-axis somewhere below y = 0. In fact, b is the value on the y-axis where the line passes through this axis. The line intercepts, or crosses, the y-axis here, and, therefore, b is called the y-intercept. Summary of Details This linear function: f(x) = mx + b May be graphed on the x, y plane as this equation: y = mx + b [Mother talking to Fraser. [blows on two magnet balls very hard before adding them to the construction on the black base] Kittens are sometimes born with defects. People who receive cats as gifts are recommended to get it examined for its health. Some birth defects, like heart problems, require urgent vet attention. Others are harmless, like polydactyly. Polydactyly means many digits, or many "fingers" from poly (many) and dactyl (digit). Sometimes, there is a mutation (change) in cat families. Most cats have only four to five toes per paw, depending on whether it is the front or back paw. These mutated cats have six, seven, and in rare cases even more. All of these cats are called "polydactyl cats". They can also be called "Hemingway cats" because author Ernest Hemingway owned some of these cats. In this video, I am going show you how to pronounce the hiragana characters (ya, yu) and (yo). Hiragana Characters "ya," "yu" and "yo"Let's begin with (ya). "Ya." (ya) is used to write (yama), which means "mountain." (yama), mountain. The next character is (yu). "Yu." (yu) is used to write (yuki), which means "snow." (yuki), snow. Next, let's pronounce (yo). "Yo." (yo) is used to write (yoru), which means "night." (yoru), night. (ya, yu, yo) "Ya," "yu," "yo." Thanks for watching. To learn more about Hiragana and Japanese, please visit About.com. " Up feet down feet here come clown feet. " By Paul Bakeman, consulting educator Introduce your child to music literacy by teaching him to recognize and draw the pitches on the lines and spaces of the treble staff. Prepare the plastic plate by cutting pieces of the electrical tape length-wise and sticking them on the plate so that they look like a musical staff. You should end up with five, thin horizontal pieces of tape, spaced an equal distance apart. The staff is generally made up of five lines and four spaces. Notes are placed on these lines and spaces to indicate pitch and rhythm. Have your child draw notes (plain, open circles work best) on the lines and spaces of the staff using a dry-erase marker. To correctly identify the notes, he will need to remember that the line notes are E, G, B, D, and F from the bottom of the staff to the top (use the mnemonic "every good boy does fine") and the space notes are F, A, C, and E from bottom to top (which spells "face"). In this activity, you'll be focusing on pitch, so just have your child draw open circles. Begin by drawing only the line notes (drawn so that the appropriate line is through the center of the circle), then do the space notes (circles are in the spaces, between the lines). Once he can draw line and space notes separately, mix them up! Be careful, because the pitches E and F are both line and space notes. Call out a note name and see how fast he can draw the note. Need more practice placing and naming notes? Check out this website: Music Theory. [Chi has picked up all the large nuts with the yellow wand] Title: How to Appreciate Techno Music Introduction: Techno music is an electronic dance music (EDM) genre that originated in Detroit, Michigan in the mid-1980s. It is characterized by repetitive percussive patterns, synthesized sounds, and a focus on DJing and club culture. If you're new to techno or find yourself struggling to appreciate its complexities, this guide will help you understand and enjoy this unique musical genre. Step 1: Understand the Origins of Techno To fully grasp techno music, it's essential to know about its roots. Research the history of techno, particularly its origins in Detroit during the post-industrial era. This knowledge will provide context for the raw, minimalist sound associated with early techno records. Familiarize yourself with pioneering artists like Juan Atkins, Derrick May, and Kevin Saunderson, also known as the Belleville Three, who are credited with inventing and developing the genre. Key Tip: Explore documentaries such as "God Said Give 'Em Drum Machines" and books like "The Record Players: Somebody must have put you in your pajamas. Simon Says Read! What you need: A picture book for each player What you do: 1. Play Simon Says with a twist. Start as usual. Say, "Simon says put your hands on your hips" and do it. Wait for your child to follow. Say and do a few more actions -- clap hands, touch toes, and so on. 2. Now say, "Simon says pick up a book." Wait for your child to follow. Then go from cover to cover, as readers do, adding a few silly mistakes he can catch: "Simon says hold the book upside down -- oops!" "Simon says now hold it the right way." "Simon says point to the title...open the cover...turn the page." "Simon says point to the picture...point to a word...point to where you start to read." "Simon says turn the next page -- oops, wrong way!" "Simon says close the book -- the end!" 3. Let your child be Simon and tell you how to read a book. Why it works: Your child becomes aware of how books and print "work." More on: Diagnosing Lds I'll leave 'em for you to sort? I need you to come and sit with Mommy. ah, which hospital did you take him to? I wanna play with the peg toy. O-o-o-obvious That You're Sick Beginning Reading Design Rationale: To learn to read and spell words, children need to know that letters stand for phonemes and spellings map out those phonemes in spoken words. Before children can match letters to phonemes, they need to be able to recognize phonemes in spoken words. Short vowels are probably the toughest phonemes to identify. This lesson will help children identify /o/ (short o). They will learn to recognize /o/ in spoken words by learning a meaningful representation and a letter symbol, and then practice finding /o/ in words. Materials: Pencil; chart with "Oliver obviously thinks the opera is optional."; Hop on Pop by Dr. Suess(Random House. Procedures: 1. Explain why new idea is valuable: "Why is it important for us to learn about the letter o and the sound /o/? It is important to be able to recognize each sound in a word so that we can read and spell the word? Why else do you think that recognizing the sounds in words is important? 2. Ask students: Raise your hand if you have ever been to the doctor before. When the doctor goes to look in your mouth, what does he say? That's right, he says, "Open up and say /o/." Let's pretend we are at the doctor's office and he is looking in our mouths, and say /o/. [Open mouth and stick out tongue as if doctor were looking in mouth.] We say /o/ so that the doctor can examine our throat. Okay, let the doctor look in your mouth: /o/. 3. Let's try a tongue twister [on chart]. "Oliver obviously thinks the opera is optional." Now everyone say it together. [Repeat twice more.] Now say it again, and this time, stretch the /o/ at the beginning of the words. "Oooliver ooobviously thinks the ooopera is oooptional." Try it again, and this time break it off the word: " /o/ liver /o/ bviously thinks the /o/ pera is /o/ ptional." 4. Now we are going to do some activities with o says /o/. I'm going to say two words and I want you to tell me which word you hear /o/ in. If I said stop and step, which one would I hear /o/ in? The answer would be stop. We are going to practice identifying the /o/ sound and make words with sound /o/. 5. Okay, let's practice. Do you hear /o/ in: shop or ship? Hat or hot? Dog or cat? Octopus or platypus? Knot or note? Great! Now we are going to practice spelling and reading words using our letterboxes. First I am going to ask you to make a word such as flop. You will place each letter that represents the sound you hear in a box. Flop - /f/ /l/ /o/ /p/. I hear /f/ first, so let's put the letter that makes the sound /f/ in the first box, f. now let's do the rest: /l/ /o/ /p/. That's l, o and p. Now you try! LBL words: dot , box , cat , mop , blob , stop, clock, slip, stomp. Now, I will put the words together for you, and I want you to read them to me. [Place f,l,o,p tiles together to make the word flop.] I'll read this one to you: this is flop. [Teacher goes through each word from LBL and has student read each one.] 6. Whole text: Now I'm going to read Hop on Pop and I want you to open your mouth and stick out your tongue every time you hear /o/. Now I want you to read Doc in the Fog to me (choral reading). Doc is a magician. Do you like magic? We'll have to read to see what neat magic tricks Doc has in store for us." Assessment: Students will be assessed on both the recognizing of /o/ in spoken words ad well as during the LBL. Students will also be provided with a worksheet with different items and item names on it and they will match the objects to their correct names. References: Fullilove, Casey. Title: How to Connect a Speaker to Your iPhone with Bluetooth Introduction: Bluetooth technology allows you to connect devices wirelessly, making it easy to enjoy your music from your iPhone on a speaker without having to deal with cords or plugs. This tutorial will guide you through the process of connecting a speaker to your iPhone using Bluetooth. By following these steps, you'll be able to listen to your favorite tunes on your speaker with ease. Step 1: Check Compatibility Ensure that both your iPhone and speaker are equipped with Bluetooth capabilities. Most modern iPhones (iPhone 3GS and later) have built-in Bluetooth support, as do most portable speakers available today. Consult the user manuals for your specific models if needed. Key tip: Make sure your speaker has the latest firmware update installed for optimal compatibility and performance. Step 2: Turn On Bluetooth on Both Devices On your iPhone, open Settings > Bluetooth and toggle the switch to enable Bluetooth. The switch should turn green, indicating that Bluetooth is active. For the speaker, refer to its manual for specific instructions on enabling Bluetooth. Typically, this involves pressing and holding the power button until an LED light starts blinking rapidly. This indicates that the device is in pairing mode. Step 3: Search for Nearby Devices Go back to your iPhone settings and make sure Bluetooth is still enabled. Underneath the 'My Devices' section, select 'Other Devices'. Wait for nearby Bluetooth devices to appear in the list below. This action puts your iPhone into discovery mode where it scans for any available Bluetooth devices within range. As soon as the speaker appears in the list, proceed to the next step. Step 4: Pair the Speaker with Your iPhone Tap on the name of your speaker in the 'Other Devices' list. If prompted for a passcode, enter it according to the information provided by the speaker manufacturer. Often, there won't be a need for a passcode, but some high-security speakers may require one. Once paired successfully, the speaker's name will move up to the 'My Devices' section under 'Bluetooth', confirming a successful connection. At this point, your speaker should emit a confirmation sound or message. Step 5: Test Playback Open your preferred audio app (e.g., Music, Spotify, Pandora), choose a song, and hit play. You should hear the sound coming out of the connected speaker instead of your iPhone itself. If no sound comes out of the speaker, double-check all connections and try restarting both devices before troubleshooting further. Additional Tips & Guidelines: * Keep your devices close together during setup and usage to maintain strong signal strength and minimize interference. * Remember that other electronic devices can interfere with Bluetooth signals; avoid placing the speaker near routers, cordless phones, microwaves, etc. * To save battery life, disconnect the speaker when it's not in use by selecting 'Forget This Device' in your iPhone's Bluetooth settings. Then reconnect whenever you want to use the speaker again. * Be aware that multiple users could potentially connect their devices to the same speaker, so ensure others aren't accidentally playing music on your speaker while trying to connect yours. Title: How to Annoy a Pookie on Club Penguin Club Penguin is a popular online multiplayer game that allows players to interact with one another through various activities, including chatting. One common term used within this community is "Pookie," which typically refers to younger or immature players who may exhibit annoying behaviors. If you're looking to prank or annoy a Pookie on Club Penguin without breaking any rules or resorting to harassment, we have compiled some harmless yet effective strategies for you. Keep in mind that these steps are intended as a lighthearted joke and should be carried out respectfully and sparingly. **Step 1: Ignore Their Messages** The first and most straightforward way to annoy a Pookie is by simply ignoring their messages. By refusing to engage with them, you effectively take away the attention they crave, making your lack of response more frustrating than if you had responded negatively. This method also ensures that no rules are broken since you aren't engaging in harmful behavior. Key tip: Be prepared to face potential questions from other users about why you're ignoring someone. Simply explain that you find the person's behavior irritating but won't stoop to their level. **Step 2: Use Predefined Phrases** Another tactic to subtly annoy a Pookie involves using predefined chat phrases instead of typing custom responses. These preset options can range from simple greetings like "Hi!" to general comments such as "That looks cool." Using these default responses repeatedly will likely confuse and potentially agitate a Pookie, especially if they were expecting personalized interactions. Guideline: Make sure your choice of predefined phrase isn't overly friendly, as doing so could unintentionally encourage further interaction with the Pookie. **Step 3: Report Rule Violations** If the Pookie engages in rule-breaking behavior, report them immediately. While this action might not directly annoy them, reporting violators contributes to maintaining a positive gaming environment, reducing the likelihood of encountering similar individuals in the future. Moreover, taking responsibility for upholding the game's rules demonstrates maturity and sets an example for others. Important note: Only report genuine rule violations; false reports damage credibility and hinder efforts to create a safer space for all users. **Step 4: Engage Them Politely (But Boringly)** Should direct confrontation become necessary, try responding politely but uninterestingly. For instance, answer every question with factual information about penguins or Arctic wildlife. Although seemingly innocent, prolonged exposure to trivia may discourage the Pookie from continuing their attempts at conversation. Key tip: Avoid sarcasm or passive-aggressiveness when employing this strategy, as it could escalate the situation rather than diffusing it. **Step 5: Change Servers** When faced with persistent nuisances, consider switching servers temporarily. Changing servers provides temporary relief from unwanted interactions while preserving peace and harmony within the virtual world of Club Penguin. It also enables continued enjoyment of the game without having to endure needless frustration caused by annoying individuals. Remember: Constantly changing servers solely to avoid specific users can lead to missed opportunities for socializing with new people and participating in enjoyable activities offered by the platform. By following these steps, you can effectively and harmlessly annoy a Pookie on Club Penguin without crossing any boundaries or compromising your integrity. Happy playing! Title: How to Change a GroupMe Topic on PC or Mac GroupMe is a popular group messaging app that allows users to create and join groups to communicate with one another. Each group can have its own unique topic to help members quickly identify its purpose or theme. If you're an admin of a GroupMe group, you may want to change the topic at some point. This tutorial will guide you through changing a GroupMe topic using either a PC or a Mac. **Time Required:** A few minutes ### **Step 1:** Open your preferred web browser Open any web browser such as Google Chrome, Mozilla Firefox, Microsoft Edge, Safari, etc., installed on your computer (PC or Mac). You don't need any specialized software other than what comes pre-installed with most operating systems. *Using a web browser instead of the mobile application provides better visibility and ease when editing text.* ### **Step 2:** Navigate to the GroupMe website In the address bar of your chosen web browser, type "[www.groupme.com](http://www.groupme.com)" and press Enter. The official GroupMe website should load up. *Accessing the GroupMe platform via their website ensures consistent functionality without worrying about limitations imposed by different mobile operating systems.* ### **Step 3:** Sign in to your account Click on 'Sign in' located at the top right corner of the homepage. Then enter your phone number or email address associated with your GroupMe account along with the password. Click 'Next', then click 'Yes, this is me'. After successful verification, you will be redirected to the main page where all your chats are displayed. *Ensure you remember your login credentials since they grant access to sensitive information related to your communication channels within GroupMe.* ### **Step 4:** Access the desired group Locate the group whose topic you wish to modify from the list of conversations shown on the left side panel. Once found, simply click on it to open the chat window. *If you cannot find the specific group immediately, try scrolling down the conversation list or utilizing the search function provided on the top right corner of the screen.* ### **Step 5:** Switch to the Admin View At the upper part of the opened group chat, look for three dots arranged horizontally ('...'). Hover over them until a dropdown menu appears. From there, select 'Admin view'. *Switching to Admin View grants additional administrative privileges like modifying the group settings including the topic.* ### **Step 6:** Edit the Group Topic After switching to Admin View, locate the current group topic just below the group name near the center of the page. To edit it, click directly onto the existing text which turns into an editable field. Input the new topic you desire here. Remember, topics must adhere to Community Guidelines. *Choosing an informative yet concise topic enables easier recognition among multiple groups and encourages active participation.* ### **Step 7:** Save Changes Once satisfied with the newly typed topic, hit 'Enter' or click anywhere outside the editable area to save changes automatically. Upon saving, the updated topic becomes visible to all group participants instantly. *Double check spelling and grammar before hitting 'Enter' to avoid confusion among group members.* That concludes our comprehensive tutorial on changing a GroupMe topic on both PC and Mac platforms! We hope these clear steps assist you in managing your GroupMe groups effectively. Happy communicating! That's what he [Bro] was doing in the store. Disposing of a Flat Screen TV: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: Disposing of an old or damaged flat screen television can be a daunting task due to its size and environmental impact. However, with careful planning and consideration, you can ensure that your TV is disposed of properly while minimizing harm to the environment. This guide will provide you with a comprehensive set of steps to help you through this process. Step 1: Determine if Your Television Still Works Before disposing of your flat-screen TV, determine whether it still functions. If so, consider selling it online, donating it to charity, or repurposing it within your home. Many organizations accept working electronics as donations, providing them to those who cannot afford new devices. Selling your functioning television also reduces waste by keeping it out of landfills. Key Tip: Always wipe down and clean your TV before attempting to sell or donate it. Make sure all personal information has been removed from any built-in smart features. Step 2: Check Local Regulations and Guidelines Familiarize yourself with local regulations regarding electronic waste (e-waste). Some areas have specific rules about recycling e-waste and may impose fines for improper disposal. Contact your city's waste management department or visit their website to learn more about these regulations. They might offer special pickup days or drop-off locations for such items. Guideline: Keep track of relevant phone numbers, websites, and addresses related to e-waste disposal in your area for future reference. Step 3: Find a Certified Electronic Recycler If your TV does not work or if there are no other options available locally, find a certified electronic recycler near you. These facilities safely handle and recycle various types of e-waste, ensuring minimal negative impact on the environment. Websites like Earth911 or e-Stewards can assist you in locating reputable e-recyclers close by. Key Tip: Choose a recycler that follows strict data security protocols when handling used equipment containing sensitive information. Step 4: Prepare Your Television for Transportation Unplug your TV from the wall outlet and remove any cables attached to it. Use packing materials, blankets, or towels to wrap and protect the delicate parts of the screen during transportation. Be cautious when lifting and carrying heavy objects; ask someone for assistance if needed. Safety Guideline: Never lift a large object alone if it causes strain or discomfort. Seek help to avoid injury. Step 5: Deliver the Television to the Designated Location Bring your prepared flat-screen TV to the chosen e-waste recycling facility or arrange for a scheduled pickup if offered. Provide necessary identification documents, proof of residence, or other required paperwork upon arrival. Follow any additional instructions provided by staff members at the facility. Environmental Guideline: Refrain from throwing away batteries, light bulbs, or small electronic components along with your television. Properly dispose of these separately according to local regulations. Conclusion: Properly disposing of a flat-screen TV requires some effort but ultimately contributes significantly to protecting our environment. By following these steps and utilizing certified electronic recyclers, you can feel confident knowing that your device will be handled responsibly and safely. Sue I bit a hole in it.
