This activity can be done solo or with friends, either online or offline. It requires creativity, patience, and dedication to fully immerse yourself into this fantasy world. Here are some general tips before we begin: 1. Establish your character's background story, motivations, skills, weaknesses, etc. 2. Step 1: Research and Preparation Time Required: Varies depending on individual research speed and material availability. curious about how sailboats work and asks "Why does a sailboat go forward if the wind is pushing from the side?" |It is fairly simple to explain how a sailboat sails when going downwind. (When the wind is behind you.) You simply let the sails out as far as they will go and the wind pushes on them and the boats hull follows along. However, when the wind is coming from the side there are some special physics at work. The force that the wind transfers to the sails actually makes a boat move forward for much the same reason a plane flies. If you were to look down on a sailboat from a helicopter you would see what looks like an airplane's wing, except standing on end. The air moving across the sails, like air moving across an airplane wing, creates a force called lift. A small amount of this force aims aft and actually pushes the boat forward and some of the force is lost due to friction as it moves over the sail. However, most of the force is sideways which tends to make the boat move sideways away from the wind. The keel or centerboard keeps the boat from being pushed sideways by the wind. The resistance from the hull and the keel translate the lifting force to forward motion. |If you did not have a keel or centerboard the boat would simply move sideways away from the wind. However, with a well-shaped keel the boat will sail mainly forward while sliding slightly to leeward (away from the direction the wind is blowing). The force works on the keel like a thumb on an orange seed. If you put an orange seed on a table and place your thumb on it and push down, it will squirt forward. The keel, which should be shaped similar to the orange seed, does the same thing when a sideways force is placed on it.| Back to The Home Page Copyright 1998/2009 Nautical Know How, Inc. Title: Adding Up Odd Numbers with a Twist! Have you ever heard of odd numbers? They are the numbers that cannot be paired up easily because they leave a "single one out." Examples include 1, 3, 5, 7, and so on. You may notice something special about them - each time you add two odd numbers together, you get another odd number! Now, let's try something fun. What do you think is the sum of the first ten odd numbers? To make it easier, here are the first ten odd numbers listed: 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15, 17, and 19. Can you add them all up? It would take some time, wouldn't it? But don't worry, there is a shortcut to finding the sum without adding every single one of them! Here is where things get exciting. Let's say your teacher asks you to find the sum of the first twenty or even thirty odd numbers. That sounds impossible without spending too much time, right? Not anymore! I am going to share a secret trick with you using a concept called patterns. We'll discover a pattern in the way odd numbers increase when added together. This method works not just for ten numbers but any given number! How cool is that? Pattern Power! ---------------- When you start adding the first few odd numbers, you might observe a pattern emerging: 1 = 1^2 (that's squaring the number) 1 + 3 = 2^2 1 + 3 + 5 = 3^2 1 + 3 + 5 + 7 = 4^2 ...and so on. It seems that by placing consecutive odd numbers in a sequence, their sum forms the square of the count of those very numbers! In other words, the sum of the first n odd numbers is equal to n (pronounced "n squared"). Using this new knowledge, we no longer need to manually add long lists of odd numbers; instead, multiply the desired quantity of odds by itself! Making Sums Quick! ------------------ Want to know the sum of the first fifty odd numbers? Just square 50! With practice, you could calculate the answer almost instantly: 50 = 2500. Try solving more challenging problems like computing the sum of the first hundred odd numbers or even five hundred! See how fast you become with this fantastic trick! Remember, learning to recognize and utilize patterns will open doors to quicker problem-solving techniques in various areas of mathematics. Happy calculating! Let us use the child from the last example. We will call her "Mary". Mary has brown hair but has genes for both red and brown hair. Let us say Mary grew up and married Tom. Tom also has brown hair, but like Mary one of his parents had red hair. This means Tom has genes for both red and brown hair. Mary and Tom would each have a chance of passing either brown or red hair genes to their children. This means that the children of Mary and Tom could have either red or brown hair. This explains why a person might look different from their parents, but look like their grandparents or great grandparents. People might meet
